items.title: Hexagonal architecture
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Hexagonal architecture in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Creating ports for every possible adapter — defining 20 interfaces (UserReader, UserWriter, UserNotifier, UserValidator) before any adapter exists. The ports should emerge from the adapters the application actually needs, not predicted upfront. Start with one interface per adapter type (UserRepository, EmailSender, PaymentGateway) and add more as needed.

Letting the domain depend on framework types — a domain service that imports gin.Context, *sql.DB, or prometheus.Counter. The domain should be pure Go with zero framework imports. Framework dependencies belong in the adapter layer, not the domain.

Making hexagonal architecture an all-or-nothing choice — applying hexagonal architecture to every package, including utility functions and configuration reading. Hexagonal architecture is for the domain core: business logic, entities, and use cases. Configuration, CLI parsing, and logging setup are infrastructure and do not need ports and adapters.
execution_timeline: Team builds an order management system with hexagonal architecture
→ Domain core: internal/domain/order.go — type Order struct { ... } with business methods
→ Port interface: internal/domain/order.go also defines type OrderRepository interface { Save(ctx, Order) error; FindByID(ctx, OrderID) (Order, error) }
→ Inbound adapter: internal/adapters/http/order_handler.go — parses HTTP request, calls domain service, formats response
→ Outbound adapter: internal/adapters/postgres/order_repo.go — implements OrderRepository with SQL queries
→ Test: create a mock OrderRepository in the test package — tests the domain service without a database
→ New requirement: add a gRPC endpoint for order creation
→ Team adds: internal/adapters/grpc/order_server.go — implements gRPC service, calls same domain service
→ No domain changes: the domain does not know about HTTP or gRPC — it only knows about ports (interfaces)
→ New infrastructure: switch from Postgres to MongoDB
→ Team adds: internal/adapters/mongodb/order_repo.go — implements same OrderRepository interface with MongoDB driver
→ One-line change in main.go: switch from postgres.NewOrderRepo to mongodb.NewOrderRepo — domain unchanged
failure_modes: Port interface defined in adapter package — the OrderRepository interface is in internal/adapters/postgres/ and the domain imports it. This creates a dependency from domain → infrastructure, violating the Dependency Inversion Principle. Fix: the port interface must be defined in the domain package; the adapter imports the domain and implements the interface.

Adapters leak infrastructure concerns into domain errors — the postgres adapter returns sql.ErrNoRows which the domain must import to handle. Fix: the adapter must map infrastructure errors to domain errors (NotFoundError, ConflictError, InternalError) defined in the domain package.

Too many layers in the adapter — an HTTP handler goes through: adapter/http/handler → adapter/http/middleware → adapter/http/request → adapter/http/response → service. Each layer adds indirection without value. Fix: keep adapters thin — the handler should parse the request, call the domain service, and format the response. No business logic in the adapter.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify the dependency direction violation given a diagram: domain imports infra/postgres for the OrderRepository interface — the interface must be in the domain package, not the infra package.

Learner must refactor a codebase that has business logic in HTTP handlers by extracting the domain, creating port interfaces, implementing adapters, and verifying no domain code imports infrastructure packages.
Learner must explain why swapping a Postgres adapter for a MongoDB adapter requires changing main.go but not the domain — the domain depends on the interface, not the implementation.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Hexagonal architecture in Go follows the Dependency Inversion Principle: domain/entity packages define port interfaces (interfaces that represent what the domain needs from the outside world). Adapter packages implement those interfaces. The domain never imports an adapter package — it only imports its own types and standard library types. The wiring happens in main.go, which imports both domain and adapter packages and passes concrete adapters to domain constructors. Go's implicit interface satisfaction (duck typing) makes this pattern ergonomic: the adapter does not need to import the interface — it just needs to have methods with matching signatures.
zero_magic.mental_model: Hexagonal architecture is a power outlet. The domain is the device (lamp, phone charger). The port is the outlet shape (Type A, Type C). The adapter is the plug that connects the device to the outlet. The device does not know what power source is behind the outlet (grid, generator, battery). The outlet does not know what device is plugged in. The adapter translates between the device's needs and the source's capabilities. In Go: the domain defines the outlet (interface). The adapter (Postgres repo, HTTP handler) implements the outlet shape. main.go is the electrician that wires them together.
performance_implications: Interface dispatch through hexagonal ports costs ~1ns per call — the same as any Go interface call. No runtime overhead from the pattern itself.
Adapter-level marshaling/unmarshaling (JSON ↔ domain types, SQL rows ↔ domain types) adds 1-10µs per operation. This is the cost of the adapter translating between infrastructure and domain formats.
The main cost of hexagonal architecture is cognitive: developers must navigate more packages and understand the port-adapter distinction. This is paid once during development, not at runtime.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go applications tie business logic directly to specific infrastructure — an HTTP handler that writes to Postgres and sends emails via SendGrid. Changing the database or email provider requires rewriting handlers. Hexagonal architecture separates business logic (domain) from infrastructure (adapters). The domain defines what it needs (ports). Adapters implement those ports. Changing infrastructure means writing a new adapter and changing one line in main.go — the domain is untouched.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner is given a Go service with business logic mixed in HTTP handlers and direct Postgres calls. They must: (1) extract domain types and business logic into an internal/domain package, (2) define port interfaces in the domain package, (3) implement adapters for HTTP (inbound) and Postgres (outbound), (4) wire everything in main.go, (5) write a test that mocks the outbound adapter and tests domain logic without any infrastructure.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Hexagonal architecture is the standard pattern for Go services that need to outlive their initial infrastructure choices. Uber's Go monorepo uses hexagonal architecture for critical payment and pricing services. Monzo's Go backend uses ports and adapters for banking core services. Kubernetes' controller-runtime library follows the hexagonal pattern: controllers define ports (reconcile interfaces), and adapters implement them for different infrastructure backends.
step_by_step_execution: Identify the domain core: business entities (Order, User, Product) and business rules (canCancelOrder, calculateTax)
Create internal/domain/ package with domain types, port interfaces, and service methods
Define port interfaces in the domain: type OrderRepository interface { Save(ctx, Order) error }; type PaymentGateway interface { Charge(ctx, Payment) error }
Create inbound adapters: internal/adapters/http/ — handler parses HTTP, calls domain service, formats response
Create outbound adapters: internal/adapters/postgres/ — implements OrderRepository with SQL
Wire in main.go: repo := postgres.NewOrderRepo(db); svc := domain.NewOrderService(repo); handler := http.NewOrderHandler(svc)
Verify: grep -r "import.*adapters" internal/domain/ should return nothing — domain has no adapter imports
Test: inject a mock OrderRepository into domain service — tests run in milliseconds, no database needed
Swap infrastructure: implement MongoDB adapter, change one import in main.go — domain unchanged
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go's implicit interface satisfaction is what makes hexagonal architecture possible without annotations or code generation. An adapter type satisfies a domain port interface simply by having methods with the right names and signatures. The compiler checks this at the point of assignment: var repo domain.OrderRepository = postgres.NewOrderRepo(db) — if postgres.OrderRepo does not have the right methods, the assignment fails to compile. This is a compile-time check with zero runtime cost. The adapter package imports the domain package (to access the interface and domain types), but the domain package never imports the adapter package. This creates the desired dependency direction: infrastructure depends on domain, not the other way around.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Hexagonal architecture exists because infrastructure is volatile and business logic is stable. Database schemas change, cloud providers are swapped, and message queues are replaced — but the business rules (an order can only be canceled if it has not shipped) stay the same for years. Hexagonal architecture ensures that volatile infrastructure changes do not disturb stable business logic. When the team switches from Postgres to CockroachDB, they write a new adapter — the domain code is already correct and tested. Without hexagonal architecture, infrastructure changes require rewriting business logic, which introduces bugs in the most valuable part of the codebase.

items.title: Domain modeling
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Domain modeling in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Making domain types anemic — a User struct with only fields and getters/setters. All business logic lives in services. This is not domain modeling — it is data structures with no behavior. Domain types should encapsulate business rules: user.Cancel() should enforce that the user cannot be canceled after 30 days.

Using primitive obsession — representing OrderStatus as a string, Money as a float64, UserID as an int. The compiler cannot catch type errors: passing an OrderID where a UserID is expected compiles fine. Fix: use value types: type OrderID string, type Money struct { amount Decimal; currency Currency }.

Modeling the database schema instead of the domain — the User struct has CreatedAt, UpdatedAt, DeletedAt timestamps because the DB table has them, but the domain does not need them. The User domain type should only have fields that the business logic needs. Database concerns (timestamps, version numbers) belong in the repository mapping.
execution_timeline: Team models an Order in an e-commerce system
→ Naive approach: type Order struct { ID string; Status string; Total float64; Items []Item; CreatedAt time.Time }
→ Problem: status is a string — typo 'shipped' vs 'shipped' is a runtime bug. Total is float64 — floating point precision errors for money
→ Refactored: type OrderStatus string with const (OrderPending OrderStatus = "pending"; OrderShipped OrderStatus = "shipped")
→ Refactored: type Money struct { Amount int64; Currency string } — stored as cents, no floating point
→ Business rule: an order can only be canceled if status is pending and created less than 1 hour ago
→ Domain method on Order: func (o *Order) CanCancel() bool { return o.Status == OrderPending && time.Since(o.CreatedAt) < 1*time.Hour }
→ Domain method: func (o *Order) Cancel() error { if !o.CanCancel() { return ErrOrderCannotBeCanceled }; o.Status = OrderCanceled; return nil }
→ The Cancel method enforces the business rule — no service code can accidentally cancel an order that should not be canceled
→ Test: order.Cancel() after 61 minutes returns ErrOrderCannotBeCanceled — the rule is tested on the domain type itself
failure_modes: Domain types use interface{} or any for fields — type Order struct { Metadata any } lets any value be stored. The service must type-assert at runtime to use Metadata. Fix: use specific types. If the metadata structure varies, use a type-safe interface: type Metadata interface { Validate() error }.

Domain types are mutable everywhere — Order fields are public and can be modified from any package. A service accidentally sets order.Status = "shipped" on an order that is still pending. Fix: make fields unexported and provide behavior methods: order.Ship() can validate that the order is paid before shipping.

Over-aggressive modeling — every concept becomes a value type: Address, PhoneNumber, EmailAddress, PostalCode, CreditCard, ExpirationDate, CVV. The codebase has 50 value types and the developer spends more time converting between types than writing business logic. Fix: use value types only for concepts that have validation rules or behavior. Simple wrappers (type Name string) add ceremony without value.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify which of two modelings is better for a given domain (e.g., a medical records system vs. a blog CMS) — medical records need strong type safety (type DiagnosisCode string with validation), blog CMS can use primitive types (tags []string).

Learner must refactor a primitive-typed model (string status, float64 price, int userID) into a domain-modeled version with value types and behavior methods, and verify that invalid states become unrepresentable.
Learner must explain why a domain type with public fields and no methods is a code smell — it means the business logic is elsewhere, making it harder to enforce invariants and harder to test.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go domain modeling uses concrete types with behavior methods. A domain type (Order) has unexported fields and exported methods that encapsulate business rules. Value types (Money, OrderID, OrderStatus) are defined as distinct types from their underlying primitives to prevent accidental mixing. Interfaces are used for domain capabilities (type InvoiceGenerator interface { Generate(Order) Invoice }). The domain package has zero imports from outside the standard library — it depends only on types it defines and standard library types (time, error, context).
zero_magic.mental_model: Domain modeling is making illegal states unrepresentable. If an Order cannot have a Shipped status without a ShipDate, then the Order type should not have a Status field and a ShipDate field independently. Instead, the Order should have a union-like structure: type Order struct { Pending *PendingOrder; Shipped *ShippedOrder }. Or simpler: provide methods that enforce the invariant: order.Ship(now) sets both Status and ShipDate atomically. The goal is that no valid Go program can create an invalid domain state.
performance_implications: Value types (type OrderID string) have zero runtime cost — they are the same size as the underlying type. Method calls on value types through interfaces cost ~1ns dispatch.
Domain method calls (order.Cancel(), order.Ship()) cost one function call per method — negligible. The business logic inside the method (date comparisons, status checks) dominates.

Validation on construction (NewOrder validates all fields) costs one pass over the fields — a few microseconds. This is a one-time cost per entity creation and prevents invalid entities from existing in the system.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go developers model the database, not the domain. Their types mirror SQL tables: User, Order, Product are structs with all fields exported, no methods, and no validation. Business rules are scattered across handlers and services. When a rule changes (orders can only be canceled within 1 hour instead of 24 hours), the developer must find every place that checks this rule. Domain modeling puts the rules ON the types: order.Cancel() checks the rule in one place. When the rule changes, only the domain type changes.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner is given a Go codebase with anemic domain types (User, Order, Product with only fields, all exported) and business rules scattered across HTTP handlers. They must: (1) identify all business rules (e.g., order total must be positive, user email must be unique, product price cannot be negative), (2) move the rules onto domain types as methods, (3) add value types for domain primitives (OrderID, Money, EmailAddress with validation), (4) verify that invalid states are unrepresentable or guarded by construction, (5) demonstrate that the refactored domain has zero imports from outside the standard library.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Domain modeling in Go is used by projects where business rules are complex and change frequently. Kubernetes models its API resources as domain types with validation methods (Validate(), Mutate()) on the types themselves. Docker's engine models images, containers, and volumes with domain methods for lifecycle operations. Monzo's banking core models accounts, transactions, and payments with strong domain types that prevent invalid financial states. The pattern: when the business logic is complex, put it on the types.
step_by_step_execution: Identify the core domain concepts: Order, User, Product, Payment
Define value types for primitives: type OrderID string, type Money struct { cents int64; currency string }, type Email string with validation
Define domain structs with unexported fields: type Order struct { id OrderID; status OrderStatus; total Money; createdAt time.Time }
Add constructors: func NewOrder(id OrderID, total Money) (*Order, error) — validates inputs, returns error for invalid state
Add behavior methods: func (o *Order) Cancel() error — enforces business rules, returns error if cancel is not allowed
Remove public field access — all state changes go through methods that enforce invariants
Test the domain: create orders in various states, call methods, verify that rules are enforced — tests are pure Go, no database or network needed
Verify: cross-reference the business requirements document — every rule should have a corresponding domain method or constructor validation
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go's type system supports domain modeling through: (1) type definitions (type OrderID string) that create distinct types with no implicit conversions; (2) method declarations on any named type; (3) unexported fields that prevent external mutation; (4) constructor functions (NewOrder) that validate invariants at creation time; (5) error return values that make invalid operations explicit. Go does not have algebraic data types (Rust's enum, Haskell's sum types), so domain modelers use the 'validate on construction' pattern: accept all inputs in a constructor, validate business rules, return error if invalid, and return a valid entity if all rules pass. Once constructed, the entity's methods maintain the invariant.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Domain modeling exists because primitive types (string, int, float64) are too permissive. An Email string can be 'not-an-email'. An OrderStatus string can be 'SHIPPEDD'. A Money float64 can have $0.1 rounding error. Domain types restrict what values are possible: type Email string with NewEmail() validation ensures only valid emails exist. type OrderStatus string with defined constants ensures only valid statuses exist. type Money struct with int64 cents ensures no floating-point errors. The goal is to use the type system to prevent bugs at compile time instead of catching them at runtime.

items.title: Invariants
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Invariants in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Assuming the database enforces all invariants — application-level invariants (e.g., 'order total equals sum of line items') are not expressible as database constraints and must be checked in code.
Only checking invariants at write time, never at read time — a data migration, backfill, or manual DB edit can create invariant violations that are invisible until the data is read months later.
Checking invariants in the wrong layer — validation in the handler passes, but the service layer has a different code path that bypasses validation, creating inconsistent state.

Concurrent invariant violation — two goroutines read the same balance (both see $100), both approve a $90 withdrawal, and both write $10 — the invariant 'balance >= 0' is violated because the second write was based on stale data.
Using panic for invariant violations in library code — an invariant check in a library panics the entire program instead of returning an error, making recovery impossible.
execution_timeline: User sends POST /withdraw with amount $90 and account ID 42
→ Handler calls service.Withdraw(42, 90)
→ Service reads account 42 from DB: balance = $100 (READ COMMITTED snapshot at time T1)
→ Service checks invariant: balance (100) >= amount (90) → true, proceed
→ Service computes new balance: 100 - 90 = 10
→ Service writes UPDATE accounts SET balance=10 WHERE id=42 — this holds a row-level lock on account 42
→ Service commits transaction — writes are visible to other transactions, lock is released
→ Concurrent withdrawal: another request reads account 42 at time T2 (before the first tx committed) — balance = $100 (stale read at REPEATABLE READ, or the read happened before the first commit)
→ Second tx also checks invariant: 100 >= 80 → true, computes new balance = 20
→ Second tx writes UPDATE accounts SET balance=20 WHERE id=42 — overwrites the first write! Final balance = $20, should be $10
→ Invariant violated: the account allowed $170 in withdrawals from a $100 balance
failure_modes: TOCTOU race: two goroutines read the invariant guard simultaneously, both pass, both write — the write order determines the final state, and both operations' preconditions are invalidated by the other.

Migration-invariant drift: a database migration adds a column that makes an existing invariant invalid (e.g., adding is_premium to an account without enforcing that premium accounts have a valid credit card on file) — existing rows violate the new invariant silently.
Deferred invariant check: an invariant is checked at request time but the actual write happens asynchronously — by the time the write executes, the invariant condition may no longer hold.

Cross-record invariant: the invariant 'sum of all invoice line items equals invoice total' requires reading all line items — if a line item is inserted between the read and the write, the invariant is checked against stale data.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify the implicit invariant in a code sample before the lesson names it.
Learner must write a test that would catch an invariant violation before seeing the fix.
Learner must design an invariant check for a multi-row constraint (e.g., 'order total = sum(line_item_totals)') and identify the race condition in a naive implementation.
Learner must choose the correct isolation level (READ COMMITTED vs REPEATABLE READ vs SERIALIZABLE) for a given invariant and explain why the weaker levels allow violations.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's standard library enforces invariants through its APIs: map access without initialization panics (invariant: map must be initialized before use), interface conversions check the type tag (invariant: type assertion must match), and io.Reader contracts (invariant: Read may return n > 0 with io.EOF). In Go services, invariants are enforced at the service layer (before writing to DB) and at the DB layer (CHECK constraints, unique indexes, foreign keys). Go's explicit error handling forces the developer to decide what happens when an invariant is violated — the error must be handled, not silently ignored.
zero_magic.mental_model: An invariant is a truth about your data that must always hold. It is not a goal — it is a constraint. 'Account balance >= 0' is an invariant. 'Accounts should generally have positive balances' is a goal. Invariants are enforced with checks at every write path, ideally at the database level (CHECK constraints) or at the service layer with a mutex or serialized access. Every read path should also check invariants defensively — stale data from migrations or manual edits can violate invariants silently.
performance_implications: Database-level CHECK constraints are nearly free (the DB evaluates them atomically with the write) — use them for single-row invariants.

Multi-row invariants (e.g., 'sum of line items = total') require a read before write — this adds latency and creates a window for race conditions; use SERIALIZABLE isolation or application-level locks.
In-memory invariant checks (mutex-protected) cost ~50ns per check — use for hot-path invariants that cannot be expressed in SQL.
Cross-service invariants (e.g., 'inventory.allocated <= inventory.total for every SKU') require distributed transactions or saga patterns — these are expensive and should be minimized.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go developers write business logic that assumes invariants are maintained by the database or by implicit convention. They do not add explicit invariant checks, and they do not consider concurrent access. The first time a race condition violates an invariant, the data is corrupted and the bug is invisible until a customer reports an impossible balance. This lesson makes invariants explicit: every write path must check preconditions atomically with the write, and every read path should defensively verify invariants.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement a bank transfer function that: (1) reads both account balances, (2) checks the invariant that the sender has sufficient funds, (3) performs the transfer in a transaction with SELECT FOR UPDATE to lock both rows, (4) verifies the invariant after the transfer. They must write a concurrent test (using goroutines and sync.WaitGroup) that proves the naive implementation (without locking) violates the invariant under concurrent access.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Invariant enforcement is the foundation of data integrity in every production system. Financial systems enforce 'account balance >= 0' and 'sum of transactions = balance change'. E-commerce systems enforce 'inventory allocated <= inventory available' and 'order total = sum of line item totals'. Booking systems enforce 'seat is allocated to at most one booking'. Every violation of these invariants is a data corruption incident that requires manual reconciliation.
step_by_step_execution: Define invariant: balance >= 0 for account with ID 42
Write first version: read balance, check >= 0, subtract amount, write new balance — THIS IS BROKEN under concurrency
Write concurrent test: two goroutines both withdraw from account 42 — prove that both can pass the invariant check based on stale reads
Fix with SELECT FOR UPDATE: BEGIN; SELECT balance FROM accounts WHERE id = 42 FOR UPDATE; check invariant; UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 90 WHERE id = 42; COMMIT;
Verify fix: run the concurrent test again — only one withdrawal succeeds, the second fails with 'insufficient funds'
Add defensive read check: on every read of account 42, verify balance >= 0 — catches data corruption from migrations or manual DB edits
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Database isolation levels control how concurrent transactions interact. READ COMMITTED (default in PostgreSQL) shows only committed data but allows non-repeatable reads — two reads in the same transaction may see different values. REPEATABLE READ prevents non-repeatable reads but allows phantom reads. SERIALIZABLE prevents all concurrency anomalies but requires retry on serialization failure. SELECT FOR UPDATE locks the selected rows, preventing concurrent writes and concurrent SELECT FOR UPDATE from other transactions. The lock is held until the transaction commits or rolls back. In Go, use db.BeginTx with a context and sql.TxOptions{Isolation: sql.LevelSerializable} for the strongest isolation, but be prepared to retry on serialization errors.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Invariants exist because concurrent systems cannot assume exclusive access to data. Without explicit invariant enforcement, two concurrent requests can both read the same initial state, both validate their preconditions, and both write conflicting results — producing logically impossible states. Invariant checks at the database level (CHECK constraints, unique indexes, foreign keys) and at the application level (SELECT FOR UPDATE, optimistic locking, serializable transactions) are the only defense against data corruption in concurrent systems.

items.title: Event-driven basics
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Event-driven basics in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Publishing events directly from the handler — the handler publishes an 'order.created' event during HTTP request processing. If the event publish fails (broker is down), the request returns an error even though the database write succeeded. Events should be published after the transaction commits, not during.

Assuming events are delivered exactly once — message brokers guarantee at-least-once delivery, not exactly-once. An event may be delivered twice if the broker crashes between publishing and acknowledging. Consumers must be idempotent: processing the same event twice produces the same result.

Coupling event format to internal types — publishing a Go struct as JSON means changing the struct field name breaks all consumers. Events are a contract: define a schema (protobuf, Avro, or a versioned JSON schema) and map between internal types and event types.
execution_timeline: User places an order → handler calls OrderService.PlaceOrder()
→ Service writes order to DB: INSERT INTO orders ...
→ Service publishes event to broker: 'order.created' with order ID, user ID, total
→ Email service consumes event: sends order confirmation email to user
→ Inventory service consumes event: decrements allocated stock
→ Analytics service consumes event: records order for reporting
→ All three consumers process the same event independently — no direct coupling between them
→ If email service is down, the event stays in the queue — it processes when email service recovers
→ If inventory service crashes mid-processing, the event is redelivered — idempotency ensures no double-decrement
failure_modes: Event ordering — a 'user.deleted' event arrives before 'order.created' for that user because the delete was published but the create was still in-flight. The order service rejects the order because the user does not exist. Fix: use outbox pattern — events are stored in the same DB transaction as the write and published reliably by a separate process.

Event schema evolution — an event field is renamed from 'total' to 'amount' in version 2. Consumers still running version 1 ignore the unknown field and process the event without the total. Fix: use schema registry and allow backward-compatible changes only (add fields, never remove or rename).

Event storm — a user update triggers 5 events: user.updated, subscription.updated, team.updated, billing.updated, notification.sent. Each event triggers more events. The system enters an event loop that overwhelms the broker. Fix: limit event cascade depth and use synchronous processing for critical paths.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify which operations should be synchronous vs. event-driven given a user registration flow: creating the user (sync, must return immediate result) vs. sending welcome email (async, can be event-driven).
Learner must explain why at-least-once delivery requires idempotent consumers and design a deduplication strategy using an event ID + processed_events table.

Learner must implement an outbox pattern: events are written to an outbox table in the same DB transaction as the business write, and a separate publisher goroutine reads from the outbox and publishes to the broker.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go services use message brokers (RabbitMQ, Kafka, NATS, Google Pub/Sub) for event-driven communication. Events are published as protobuf or JSON messages. Consumers are goroutines that subscribe to topics/queues and process events. Go's concurrency model (goroutines + channels) maps naturally to the event-driven pattern: each event handler runs in its own goroutine, and channels buffer incoming events. The standard library's encoding/json and google.golang.org/protobuf are used for event serialization. Consumer libraries provide automatic retry, dead-letter queues, and rebalancing.
zero_magic.mental_model: Event-driven architecture is a party where services talk through a megaphone (the broker) instead of private phone calls (direct API calls). Service A shouts 'Order Created!' into the megaphone. Services B, C, and D hear it and do their thing. Service A does not know or care who is listening. If Service B is in the bathroom (down), it will hear the message when it comes back (event persistence). The megaphone ensures every message is heard at least once. Each service must be prepared to hear the same message twice (at-least-once delivery) and handle it gracefully (idempotency).
performance_implications: Publishing an event to Kafka adds 1-10ms latency (network round-trip + broker write). Publishing to an in-memory channel costs ~1µs.
Event consumption adds one goroutine per partition/topic — at 10 topics with 10 partitions each, that is 100 goroutines, which is negligible for Go.
Outbox pattern adds one DB write per event (INSERT into outbox table) — negligible. The outbox publisher adds one goroutine polling the outbox table at configurable intervals.
Event serialization (protobuf) adds ~1µs per event. Deserialization adds ~1µs. These are negligible for most systems but matter at >10K events/second.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go services use synchronous API calls for everything: the order service calls the email service directly, the inventory service directly, and the analytics service directly. If any of these services is slow or down, the order request fails. Event-driven architecture decouples services: the order service publishes an event and returns immediately. Other services consume the event when they are ready. This improves availability, scalability, and resilience.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement a mini event-driven system with: (1) an event publisher that writes to a channel (simulating a broker), (2) an order service that publishes 'order.created' events, (3) an email consumer that asynchronously sends confirmations, (4) an inventory consumer that decrements stock, (5) an idempotency check using a processed_events map, (6) prove that processing the same event twice does not double-decrement stock.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Event-driven architecture is the backbone of every large-scale distributed system. Kafka processes 1M+ events/second at Netflix for real-time streaming. RabbitMQ handles 10K+ messages/second for task distribution at Docker Hub. Google Pub/Sub processes events for Google Ads at planetary scale. In Go production services, event-driven communication is used for: order processing pipelines, notification systems, data replication, analytics pipelines, and workflow orchestration.
step_by_step_execution: Define event schema: type OrderCreatedEvent struct { OrderID string; UserID string; Total Money }
Create event publisher: publishes events to message broker after DB transaction commits
Create event consumers: each consumer subscribes to a topic, processes events, acknowledges on success
Implement idempotency: each consumer checks a processed_events store before processing — skip if already processed
Handle failures: if consumer panics or returns error, the broker redelivers the event
Add dead-letter queue: events that fail after 3 retries go to a DLQ for manual inspection
Test: publish an event, verify all consumers process it; simulate a consumer crash and verify redelivery
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Message brokers use a publish-subscribe model. The publisher sends a message to a topic/exchange. The broker stores the message (persistently for Kafka/PubSub, in-memory for RabbitMQ without persistence). Consumers subscribe to the topic with a consumer group. Each message is delivered to one consumer in the group (queue semantics) or all consumers (pub-sub semantics). After processing, the consumer sends an acknowledgment. If the consumer crashes before acknowledging, the broker redelivers the message to another consumer in the group. This is at-least-once delivery. To achieve exactly-once semantics, the consumer must be idempotent (processing the same message twice produces the same result). In Go, the consumer library (e.g., Sarama for Kafka, amqp for RabbitMQ) runs the consumer loop in a goroutine and calls the handler function for each message.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Event-driven architecture exists because synchronous request-response coupling makes distributed systems fragile. If service A calls B, C, and D synchronously, the availability of A is the product of the availability of B, C, and D (0.99 × 0.99 × 0.99 = 0.97 — 3% downtime for A even though each service has 99% uptime). With events, A publishes to a broker (99.99% uptime) and returns immediately. B, C, and D process the event asynchronously. A's availability is decoupled from its dependents. Events also enable temporal decoupling: B can process events hours later if it is under maintenance.

items.title: Queues
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Queues in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using a message queue as a database — storing state in queue messages and expecting to query them. Queues are for transient work distribution, not persistent storage. Messages should be treated as ephemeral: once processed, they are gone. If you need to query past events, use a database or event store.

Blocking the consumer goroutine on a slow downstream call — consumer reads a message, calls a 30-second external API, and blocks the consumer goroutine. If the consumer has 10 goroutines and all are blocked, the queue backs up. Fix: use async processing or increase goroutine count with backpressure.

Not setting a visibility timeout or TTL — a consumer takes a message but crashes without acknowledging it. The message stays invisible forever (or until the broker restarts), causing work to be lost. Fix: always set visibility timeout and message TTL. The broker redelivers after the timeout expires.
execution_timeline: Order service creates an order → publishes 'send_email' message to RabbitMQ queue
→ Email consumer polls the queue: receives the message with a delivery tag
→ Consumer processes: connects to SendGrid API, sends the email
→ Consumer acknowledges: sends basic.Ack(deliveryTag) — message is removed from the queue
→ If SendGrid is down: consumer does not ack → after 30s timeout, message reappears in the queue
→ Another consumer picks it up (or same consumer after timeout) — retry with backoff
→ After 3 failed attempts: message is moved to dead-letter queue for manual inspection
→ Queue depth metric spikes: ops team sees the dead-letter queue growing and investigates
failure_modes: Consumer processes message but crashes before acknowledging — the message is redelivered to another consumer, causing duplicate processing. Fix: make consumers idempotent (use a processed_message_id table).

Poison message: a malformed message causes every consumer to crash when processing it. The message is redelivered infinitely, blocking the queue for all other messages. Fix: set max delivery count and a dead-letter queue.

Consumer scaling mismatch: 1000 messages/second are published but consumers can only process 100/second. The queue grows unbounded and runs out of disk. Fix: use a bounded queue with backpressure or scale consumers to match throughput.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain why a queue without a dead-letter queue is dangerous — a poison message blocks the entire queue and the ops team has no way to remove it without restarting the broker.
Learner must design a retry with exponential backoff given a queue of payment processing messages: retry after 1s, 4s, 16s, 64s, then move to DLQ.
Learner must implement a bounded Go channel as an in-memory queue and explain why unbuffered channels cause deadlocks in producer-consumer setups.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go services use queues through client libraries: RabbitMQ (github.com/streadway/amqp), Kafka (github.com/segmentio/kafka-go or github.com/IBM/sarama), Amazon SQS (github.com/aws/aws-sdk-go-v2/service/sqs), Google Pub/Sub (cloud.google.com/go/pubsub). Each library provides: publish (send message to queue), consume (receive messages via callback or pull), acknowledge (mark as processed), and nack/reject (mark as failed for redelivery). Go's concurrency model maps to queues naturally: each consumer is a goroutine, and channels buffer incoming messages before processing.
zero_magic.mental_model: A queue is a to-do list. The producer adds tasks to the list. Workers pick up tasks, do the work, and mark the task as done. If a worker crashes while working, the task goes back on the list for another worker. The list has a maximum size — if it fills up, the producer must slow down (backpressure). Tasks that keep failing go to a 'lost and found' bin (dead-letter queue) for manual handling.
performance_implications: Queue publish latency: ~1ms for RabbitMQ (in-memory), ~10ms for Kafka (disk flush), ~50ms for SQS (network + HTTP).
Consumer processing: each message adds the cost of deserialization (~1µs) + business logic + ack (~1ms network round-trip).
Batch processing: consuming 100 messages in a batch reduces per-message overhead by ~10x (one network round-trip for 100 acks instead of 100 round-trips).
Queue depth governs consumer scaling: at 1000 messages/second with 100ms processing time per message, you need 100 concurrent consumers to keep the queue empty.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go developers process background work inline: sending emails, generating reports, and processing payments during the HTTP request. This makes the request slow and reduces throughput. Queues move background work out of the request path: the handler publishes a message and returns immediately. Workers process messages asynchronously. This decouples request latency from work duration and allows independent scaling of producers and consumers.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement a producer-consumer system with: (1) an in-memory channel-based queue, (2) a producer that publishes 100 messages, (3) a consumer pool (5 goroutines) that processes messages with simulated work (time.Sleep), (4) acknowledgments (simulated by deleting from a pending set), (5) a retry mechanism with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s), (6) a dead-letter queue for messages that exceed max retries, (7) prove that all 100 messages are eventually processed exactly once.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Queues are used in every production Go service that processes background work. SendGrid uses queues to send millions of emails/day. Stripe uses queues for payment processing and webhook delivery. Docker Hub uses RabbitMQ for image push notifications. The pattern is universal: any work that should not block the HTTP response goes through a queue.
step_by_step_execution: Define queue and message format: type Queue chan Message; type Message struct { ID string; Payload []byte }
Create consumer pool: for i := 0; i < 5; i++ { go consumer(queue, done) }
In consumer: msg := <-queue; err := process(msg); if err != nil { retry(msg, backoff) } else { ack(msg) }
Implement retry: after failure, wait backoff duration, then requeue or send to DLQ after max retries
Implement dead-letter queue: separate channel for messages that exceeded max retries
Add backpressure: use a buffered channel with fixed capacity; if full, producer blocks or drops messages
Test: publish 100 messages, verify all are processed exactly once, verify retries on failure, verify DLQ works
zero_magic.under_the_hood: An AMQP message (RabbitMQ) has: body (payload), properties (content-type, delivery-mode, priority, expiration), and delivery info (exchange, routing-key, redelivered, delivery-tag). The consumer receives messages via a channel (AMQP channel, not Go channel). Each message must be acknowledged or rejected. If the consumer disconnects without acknowledging, unacked messages are redelivered to another consumer. Kafka uses a pull model: consumers poll for new messages and commit offsets. The offset is the position in the partition. If the consumer crashes, it resumes from the last committed offset. At-least-once delivery is guaranteed by: the broker stores messages persistently and consumers acknowledge after processing.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Queues exist because immediate processing of every task is neither possible nor desirable. Some tasks take too long (email delivery, PDF generation), some need retries (payment processing with external gateways), and some need batching (analytics aggregation). Queues decouple the request path from the work path: the HTTP handler returns in 10ms instead of 10s, and the queue absorbs bursts of work that would overwhelm direct processing.

items.title: Retries and idempotent consumers
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Retries and idempotent consumers in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Retrying without exponential backoff — retrying every 100ms against a failing database amplifies load by 10x per second, turning a transient failure into a self-inflicted outage.
Assuming all errors are retryable — a 400 Bad Request retried 5 times is 5 wasted calls; only 5xx, timeouts, and network errors should be retried.
Not implementing idempotency keys — a payment webhook is delivered twice; without an idempotency key, the customer is charged twice and the error is invisible until the customer notices.
Retrying inside a transaction that has already rolled back — the retried operation runs outside the transaction context, creating inconsistent state between the rollback and the retry.
Using a fixed retry count without a circuit breaker — a downstream service is down for 10 minutes, every caller retries 3 times with 1-second backoff, generating 1800 failed calls instead of 600.
execution_timeline: Client sends POST /payments with idempotency key 'abc-123'
→ Server checks if key 'abc-123' has been processed: not found → proceed
→ Server calls payment gateway: request times out after 5 seconds (network blip)
→ Client does not receive response, retries with same idempotency key after 1 second
→ Server checks idempotency key again: not found (first request never completed, no record of it)
→ Server calls payment gateway again: this time succeeds, gateway charges the card
→ Server stores result with idempotency key 'abc-123' and returns 200 OK
→ First request's response arrives late: payment gateway processed it (card was charged)
→ Server checks idempotency key: FOUND! Returns cached 200 OK (does NOT charge again)
→ Customer sees one charge on their statement, not two
failure_modes: Idempotency key collision: two different requests accidentally use the same idempotency key (e.g., based on order ID + truncated timestamp) — the second request is incorrectly treated as a duplicate and returns stale data.
Idempotency store unavailability: the idempotency cache (Redis, DynamoDB) is down — every request is treated as new, and retries cause duplicate processing because the key lookup fails.
Retry amplification (retry storm): 100 callers each retry 3 times against a degraded service — 300 requests hit the service simultaneously, pushing it from degraded to fully down.

Timeout mismatch: the client retries after 1 second but the upstream takes 5 seconds to respond — every retry overlaps with an in-flight request, and all of them fail because the upstream is overloaded.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must choose between exponential backoff and fixed-interval retry for a given failure mode before the lesson reveals the tradeoffs.
Learner must identify the retry-storm risk in a naive retry loop before seeing the circuit-breaker solution.
Learner must design an idempotency key scheme for a payment flow and identify the window where duplicate requests can both miss the idempotency cache.
Learner must explain why a retry with a 5-second timeout against an upstream with a 30-second response time always fails.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's net/http has no built-in retry or idempotency — these are implemented at the application layer. The standard pattern uses a retry loop with exponential backoff + jitter: for attempts := 0; attempts < maxRetries; attempts++ { err := doRequest(); if err == nil { break }; if !isRetryable(err) { break }; time.Sleep(backoff(attempts)) }. Idempotency keys are generated per-request (UUID v4), sent as an HTTP header (Idempotency-Key), and stored in Redis or PostgreSQL with a TTL. The response is cached so duplicate requests return the same result without re-executing the operation.
zero_magic.mental_model: Retries handle transient failures (network blips, timeouts, 503s). Idempotency ensures that retries do not cause duplicate side effects. They are a pair: retries increase reliability, idempotency prevents retries from causing harm. Exponential backoff ensures that retries do not amplify load on an already-degraded system. Circuit breakers stop retrying when the downstream is known to be down — retrying against a dead service is worse than not retrying at all.
performance_implications: Idempotency key lookup adds 1-5ms per request (Redis) or 10-50ms (PostgreSQL) — negligible for most services but significant for sub-millisecond operations.
Exponential backoff with jitter spreads retries evenly over time — without jitter, all callers retry in lockstep, amplifying load at each backoff interval.
Circuit breaker state transitions (closed → open → half-open) add no per-request overhead — the state check is a single atomic load.

Caching idempotent responses consumes memory — a payment service processing 1000 requests/s with a 24-hour idempotency window stores 86M entries; use shorter TTLs or persistent storage for long windows.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go developers either do not retry at all (failing on the first transient error) or retry blindly without backoff or idempotency (amplifying failures into outages). They do not distinguish between retryable and non-retryable errors, and they do not protect against duplicate processing. This lesson covers the retry+idempotency pair: how to retry safely with exponential backoff and jitter, and how to idempotently process requests so that retries are harmless.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement an HTTP client with: (1) retry with exponential backoff + jitter (max 3 retries), (2) classification of errors into retryable (5xx, timeout, connection refused) and non-retryable (4xx), (3) idempotency key generation and header injection, (4) a server that deduplicates requests by idempotency key. They must then demonstrate that a network blip causes the client to retry but the server processes the request exactly once.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every production Go service that calls external APIs implements retries with exponential backoff and jitter. Stripe's API requires idempotency keys for all POST requests — every payment integration must implement this. Kafka consumers use idempotent processing (the consumer tracks the offset of each message; processing the same offset twice is a no-op). AWS SDK for Go implements automatic retries with exponential backoff for all service calls.
step_by_step_execution: Client generates idempotency key: UUID v4 'abc-123' for POST /payments
Client sends request with header Idempotency-Key: abc-123
Server receives request, checks idempotency store (Redis) for key 'abc-123' — not found
Server processes payment: calls Stripe API, Stripe charges card
Server stores result in idempotency store: {key: 'abc-123', status: 'completed', charge_id: 'ch_456'}
Server returns 200 OK with charge details
Client does not receive response (network blip), retries after 200ms + random jitter
Server receives retry with same Idempotency-Key: abc-123
Server checks idempotency store: FOUND key 'abc-123' with status 'completed'
Server returns cached 200 OK — does NOT call Stripe again
If server was down during first request: idempotency key was never stored, retry processes fresh after checking key is still absent
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Idempotency is typically implemented with a conditional write: INSERT INTO idempotency_keys (key, response, created_at) VALUES ($1, NULL, NOW()) ON CONFLICT (key) DO NOTHING RETURNING created_at. If the INSERT succeeds (no conflict), this is the first request — proceed with processing. If the INSERT returns a row (conflict), this is a retry — return the cached response. The key must be unique and have a TTL (24 hours is typical for payment APIs). For distributed systems, the idempotency store must be strongly consistent (single-node Redis with fsync, or PostgreSQL with synchronous replication) — an eventually-consistent store can accept duplicate writes during a network partition.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Retries exist because networks are unreliable — packet loss, timeouts, and transient errors are normal, not exceptional. Without retries, a 0.1% packet loss rate causes 0.1% of all requests to fail permanently. Idempotency exists because retries cause duplicate requests — without idempotency, every retry is a potential double charge, duplicate order, or duplicate notification. Together, retries and idempotency make distributed systems resilient to transient failures without causing data corruption.

items.title: Payment workflow design
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Payment workflow design in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Processing payments synchronously in the HTTP handler — the request blocks for the duration of the external payment gateway call (500ms-5s), occupying a goroutine and a database connection. If the gateway is slow, all handler goroutines are blocked and the service stops accepting requests. Payments should be processed asynchronously via a queue.

Not handling partial failures in multi-step payment workflows — a payment flow involves: authorize, capture, settle, notify. If the settlement succeeds but the notification fails, the payment is settled but the user never receives a receipt. Each step must have its own error handling, retry logic, and compensating action.

Storing raw payment gateway responses without mapping to domain events — storing Stripe's full JSON response as raw JSONB means the application logic depends on Stripe's API shape. If Stripe adds or removes fields, the parsing breaks. Map gateway responses to domain events (PaymentAuthorized, PaymentCaptured, PaymentFailed) at the adapter boundary.
execution_timeline: User submits credit card on checkout page → POST /checkout with payment details
→ Service validates: card number, expiry, CVV, amount, currency — client-side validation is already done
→ Service creates a PaymentIntent in 'pending' state in the database
→ Service publishes 'payment.authorize' message to queue (async!)
→ HTTP response returns immediately: 'Payment processing, you will receive a confirmation email'
→ Queue consumer picks up 'payment.authorize': calls Stripe API to authorize the charge
→ Stripe returns authorization ID: auth_12345 — payment is authorized but not yet captured
→ Consumer updates PaymentIntent status to 'authorized' with auth ID
→ Consumer publishes 'payment.capture' message to another queue or delays for settlement
→ Settlement consumer calls Stripe API to capture: auth_12345 → charge_67890
→ Consumer updates PaymentIntent to 'captured', publishes 'payment.confirmed' event
→ Email service publishes receipt: user gets confirmation email with order details
→ If any step fails: consumer retries with exponential backoff (3 retries), then moves to dead-letter queue for manual review
failure_modes: Double charge: the capture step succeeds on Stripe's side but the network response times out. The consumer retries and captures again — the user is charged twice. Fix: use idempotency keys for ALL payment gateway calls (Stripe supports Idempotency-Key header).

Partial capture: an order with 3 items has one item out of stock. The payment was authorized for the full amount but the capture should only capture for the 2 available items. Fix: authorize for the estimated maximum, capture for the actual amount. Handle partial capture in the workflow.

Payment gateway timeout during authorization: the gateway is slow but eventually succeeds. The consumer retries and triggers a second authorization — the user sees two pending charges. Fix: idempotency key ensures the second authorization is a no-op.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify the correct order of operations in a payment workflow (authorize before capture, capture before settle) and explain what happens if the order is wrong.
Learner must design a compensating transaction for a failed payment step (e.g., authorization succeeded but capture failed — void the authorization).
Learner must implement an idempotent payment capture that uses Stripe's Idempotency-Key header and verify with a test that retrying the same request does not create a duplicate charge.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Payment workflows in Go use a saga pattern: a sequence of local transactions (DB writes) and remote calls (payment gateway API calls). Each step has a compensating action for rollback. The state machine is stored in the database (PaymentIntent with status enum). Events (payment.authorized, payment.captured, payment.failed) drive the workflow via queues. Go's concurrency model (goroutines + channels) makes the async event processing natural: each step is a consumer goroutine, and the state machine transitions are explicit in the status field.
zero_magic.mental_model: A payment workflow is a state machine with retries and compensating actions. Every state transition must be: (1) persisted before execution (write the intent, then act), (2) idempotent (same transition applied twice is harmless), (3) reversible (if capture fails, void the authorization). The state machine lives in the database, not in memory — if the service restarts, it resumes from the last persisted state. Think of it as a journey: pending → authorized → captured → settled. Each step forward has a corresponding step backward (void, refund).
performance_implications: Payment gateway API calls dominate latency: 200-500ms typical, up to 10s timeout. Async processing via queue avoids blocking the HTTP request path.
State machine transitions: each transition (pending→authorized, authorized→captured) requires a DB write — ~5ms per transition. 3 transitions per payment = 15ms of DB writes, negligible.
Idempotency storage: each payment gateway call stores the idempotency key + response. For 100 payments/second, this is 100 writes/second to Redis or PostgreSQL — easily handled.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go developers process payments inline in the HTTP handler: call the payment gateway, wait for the response, update the database, and return. This makes the HTTP request slow, blocks goroutines, and has no retry mechanism or failure recovery. Payment workflows require: async processing, idempotency, state machine tracking, and compensating actions for failures. This lesson covers the complete payment workflow design for production Go services.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement a payment workflow with: (1) a PaymentIntent state machine (pending → authorized → captured → settled + failed/refunded), (2) async processing via a channel-based queue, (3) idempotent Stripe API calls (simulated), (4) retry with exponential backoff on gateway failures, (5) compensating action (void authorization if capture fails), (6) a test that proves retrying a failed capture does not create duplicate charges.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Payment workflows are the most critical path in any e-commerce or SaaS system. Stripe processes billions of dollars/day through their API. Every payment integration follows the same pattern: authorize (hold the funds), capture (charge the card), settle (transfer to merchant account). Failure at any step requires compensating actions (authorization expires after 7 days, capture can be voided, settled amounts must be refunded). Go production services use this pattern for: subscription billing, one-time purchases, marketplace payments, and invoicing.
step_by_step_execution: Create PaymentIntent struct: type PaymentIntent struct { ID string; Amount Money; Status PaymentStatus; GatewayRef string }
Define status enum: PaymentPending, PaymentAuthorized, PaymentCaptured, PaymentSettled, PaymentFailed, PaymentRefunded
Implement state machine: each transition is a method that validates the current status and calls the next step
Implement async processing: publish 'payment.authorize' event to queue → consumer calls gateway → publishes 'payment.capture'
Add idempotency: generate UUID per step, include in gateway request header, store response keyed by UUID
Add compensating actions: if capture fails → void the authorization; if refund is requested → call gateway refund API
Test: simulate gateway timeout → verify retry happens; simulate double delivery → verify idempotency prevents duplicate charges
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Payment gateways (Stripe, Adyen, Square) provide REST APIs for payment processing. The standard flow: POST /v1/payment_intents (create with amount), POST /v1/payment_intents/{id}/confirm (authorize), POST /v1/payment_intents/{id}/capture (capture). Each call supports an Idempotency-Key header: if the same key is sent within 24 hours, the gateway returns the same response without executing the operation again. In Go, the gateway client (e.g., stripe-go) generates the idempotency key automatically if not provided. The payment state machine is stored in the application database: the PaymentIntent record tracks the current status, gateway reference IDs, and idempotency keys. The workflow is driven by a scheduler or queue: consumer goroutines check for PaymentIntents in 'authorized' status and attempt capture. If the service restarts, it scans for incomplete PaymentIntents and resumes processing.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Payment workflow design exists because charging a credit card is a multi-step, failure-prone operation. A naive implementation (call gateway, charge card, return) handles none of the failure modes: network timeouts, gateway errors, duplicate charges, partial captures, or refunds. A production payment workflow is a state machine with explicit transitions, idempotent API calls, async processing, and compensating actions. Without this design, payment failures cause revenue loss (uncaptured authorizations expire), customer complaints (double charges), and manual reconciliation (finding and processing stuck payments).

items.title: Multi-tenancy architecture
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Multi-tenancy architecture in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using a single database with tenant_id on every table and assuming it is sufficient — a missing WHERE tenant_id = ? clause in a query leaks data across tenants. This is the most common multi-tenancy bug and is invisible until a tenant complains. Every query must be scoped to the tenant, and no default query should return data without tenant filtering.

Sharing connection pools across tenants — a single *sql.DB shared by all tenants means one tenant's slow query consumes all connections, starving other tenants. Each tenant should have a separate connection pool (or at least separate pool settings with max connections per tenant).

Not isolating tenant configuration — all tenants share the same feature flags, rate limits, and pricing tiers. When tenant A needs a higher rate limit, it must be added to the code. Fix: store tenant configuration in a per-tenant settings record and load it at request time.
execution_timeline: SaaS platform serves 3 tenants: Acme Corp (enterprise, 10K users), Beta Inc (startup, 50 users), Gamma LLC (free tier, 5 users)
→ Request arrives for Acme Corp: header X-Tenant-ID: acme-corp
→ Middleware extracts tenant ID, stores in context: ctx = context.WithValue(ctx, tenantKey,
→ Service reads tenant config: rate limit = 10K req/min, database = acme_db, features = [analytics, api, webhooks]
→ Repository adds tenant filter: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE tenant_id = 'acme-corp' — ALL queries include tenant_id
→ Acme's database pool is separate from Beta's — Acme's slow query does not affect Beta's performance
→ Beta Inc grows to 500 users: update tenant config, increase rate limit and DB pool size — no code change
→ Gamma LLC is inactive: reduced DB pool to minimum, rate limit at 100 req/min — saves resources for paying tenants
failure_modes: Cross-tenant data leak via cache — a Redis cache keyed by order_id without tenant_id prefix returns order data for the wrong tenant. Fix: all cache keys must include tenant_id: cache.Get(
Cross-tenant data leak via error message — a database error includes the tenant name in the error message:
. The error is returned to beta-inc's user. Fix: sanitize error messages before returning them to users.

Tenant resource starvation — one tenant consumes 90% of CPU and 90% of memory, degrading service for all other tenants. Fix: use per-tenant connection pools, rate limiting per tenant, and CPU/memory quotas (cgroups or Kubernetes resource limits per namespace).
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify the cross-tenant data leak in a code sample where a cache lookup uses only the order ID without the tenant ID prefix.
Learner must design a multi-tenant database strategy (separate database per tenant vs. shared database with tenant_id column) and explain the tradeoffs: isolation vs. operational cost.
Learner must implement a middleware that extracts the tenant ID from a request header, validates it against a tenant registry, and stores it in the context for downstream use.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Multi-tenant Go services use a tenant ID extracted from the request (header JWT claim, or subdomain) and stored in context.Context. Every database query, cache key, and external API call includes the tenant ID. Middleware validates the tenant, loads its configuration, and attaches a scoped logger (with tenant field), scoped metrics (with tenant tag), and scoped database pool to the context. The tenant-scoped *sql.DB (or *sql.Conn) is fetched from a tenant registry map: tenantDBs[tenantID].
zero_magic.mental_model: Multi-tenancy is a hotel. Each tenant is a guest in their own room. The hotel has shared spaces (lobby, elevator — shared infrastructure) but each room is private. In software: each tenant has their own data (their room), but they share the application instance (the hotel building). The key challenge is making sure Guest A never accidentally enters Guest B's room — every door (database query, cache lookup, API call) must be scoped to the correct tenant.
performance_implications: Tenant ID extraction adds ~1µs per request (reading a header). Tenant config lookup adds ~1ms (cached in memory, nil for first request).

Tenant-scoped connection pools: each pool has min/max connections. For 100 tenants with max 10 connections each, the maximum connection count is 1000 — acceptable for most databases but requires monitoring.

Shared database with tenant_id filter: the tenant_id column must be indexed on every table. Queries with tenant_id filters use the index for efficient per-tenant data access. Without the index, full table scans per tenant destroy performance.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New SaaS services start with a single tenant (the first customer). When the second customer arrives, adding multi-tenancy requires: (1) adding a tenant_id column to every table, (2) filtering every query by tenant_id, (3) isolating tenant data to prevent leaks, (4) managing tenant-specific configuration. This lesson covers the multi-tenant architecture patterns for Go services: row-level tenant isolation, separate database per tenant, and hybrid approaches.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement a multi-tenant Go HTTP service with: (1) middleware that extracts X-Tenant-ID header and stores it in context, (2) a tenant registry (in-memory map) with per-tenant configuration, (3) a repository that scopes all queries by tenant_id, (4) tests that prove Tenant A cannot access Tenant B's data even with a direct ID guess, (5) a cache layer that uses tenant-prefixed keys.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Multi-tenancy is the standard architecture for SaaS platforms. GitHub uses organization-scoped data access (each org is a tenant). Slack uses workspace-scoped data (each workspace is a tenant). Every B2B SaaS application implements multi-tenancy. The isolation level varies: low-trust tenants (competitors sharing the same platform) need separate databases; high-trust tenants (departments of the same company) can share a database with tenant_id scoping.
step_by_step_execution: Add middleware: extract X-Tenant-ID from request header, validate against tenant registry, store in context
Add tenant_id column to every table: ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN tenant_id VARCHAR(64) NOT NULL
Add index on tenant_id for every table: CREATE INDEX idx_orders_tenant_id ON orders(tenant_id)
Update all queries to include tenant_id: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE tenant_id = $1 AND id = $2
Use tenant-scoped cache keys: cache.Get(
step_by_step_execution.+ tenantID +: Add per-tenant connection pools: tenantDBs[tenantID] = connectToTenantDB(tenantID); ctx = setDB(ctx, tenantDBs[tenantID])
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go's context.Context is the mechanism for propagating tenant ID through the call chain. The tenant middleware sets the value: ctx = context.WithValue(ctx, tenantKey, tenantID). Handlers, services, and repositories extract it: ctx.Value(tenantKey).(string). The tenant registry is typically an in-memory map loaded from a database or configuration file at startup, with periodic refresh. The per-tenant connection pool is a map[string]*sql.DB where each pool is created with sql.Open with tenant-specific connection string. The pools are cached for the lifetime of the process, with health checks to reconnect on failure.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Multi-tenancy exists because SaaS platforms serve multiple customers from a single application instance. Without explicit tenant isolation, data leaks across tenants — the most severe type of security breach for a SaaS company. Every query, cache lookup, and API call must be scoped to the authenticated tenant. Multi-tenancy is not a feature — it is a security requirement that must be designed into every layer of the application.

items.title: Caching architecture
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Caching architecture in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Caching everything with the same TTL — user profile data (changes rarely) and inventory counts (change every minute) should have very different TTLs. Using a 5-minute TTL for everything means user profiles are fetched from the database on every request (no cache benefit), and inventory counts serve stale data for 5 minutes.

Not handling cache stampedes — when a cache key expires and 100 concurrent requests all miss the cache, they all hit the database simultaneously, amplifying load by 100x. Fix: use a mutex or 'probabilistic early expiration' — one request regenerates the cache, others wait for it.

Using cache as the source of truth — if Redis goes down and all cached data is lost, the service must reconstruct it from the database. If the cache is treated as the source of truth and the DB is a backup, cache loss causes data loss. Fix: the database is the source of truth; the cache is a performance optimization.
execution_timeline: Request for user profile: cache.Get(
) → MISS (profile not cached yet or expired)
→ Request holds a distributed lock: lock.Acquire(
) → acquired (first request to miss gets the lock)
→ Request queries database: SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 42 — 50ms query
→ Request stores result in cache: cache.Set(
, user, 5*time.Minute)
→ Request releases lock: lock.Release(
→ 99 concurrent requests that missed the cache: they all tried lock.Acquire and failed (lock held)
→ Those 99 requests wait: lock.Wait or retry cache.Get after 50ms — now cache has the data!
→ Subsequent requests: cache.Get(
) → HIT → 1ms response, no database query
failure_modes: Stale cache after data update (cache invalidation miss): user updates their profile → DB is updated but the cache still has the old data for 5 minutes. Fix: invalidate the cache key on every write: cache.Delete(
) after DB update. Or use write-through cache: update DB and cache in the same operation.

Cache thundering herd with multiple keys: 1000 products' caches all expire at the same time (e.g., after a bulk price update). 1000 queries hit the database simultaneously. Fix: stagger TTLs (add random jitter: TTL + rand.Intn(60) seconds).

Cache-aside vs. read-through confusion: cache-aside (application reads cache, on miss reads DB, writes cache) gives the application full control. Read-through (cache library reads DB automatically) is simpler but less flexible. Mixing both causes double-caching and inconsistent TTLs.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain why cache stampede happens and design a solution using either a mutex or probabilistic early expiration before the lesson reveals the answer.
Learner must implement a write-through cache strategy: on every DB write, update the cache synchronously so the cache never has stale data.
Learner must choose between local (in-memory) vs. distributed (Redis) cache for a given scenario and explain the tradeoff: latency vs. consistency across service instances.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go services use cache-aside pattern: cache.Get(key) returns data or miss → on miss, query DB, cache.Set(key, data, TTL), return data. Invalidation: on write, cache.Delete(key) or cache.Set(key, newData, TTL). Go's standard library has sync.Map for in-memory caching, and common packages like go-cache (in-memory) and go-redis (distributed). For cache stampede prevention, Go uses sync.Mutex or singleflight.Group (golang.org/x/sync/singleflight) to coalesce concurrent cache misses into a single database call.
zero_magic.mental_model: Cache is a fast, small memory that sits in front of a slow, large storage. Think of it like a desk drawer (cache) and a filing cabinet (database). Frequently used files are on your desk (in cache) — you grab them instantly. If a file is not on your desk, you walk to the filing cabinet (database query). If someone asks for the same file right after you put it on your desk, they get it instantly. The key insight: you must update BOTH the filing cabinet AND the desk when a file changes, or the desk has stale information.
performance_implications: In-memory cache (Go map with sync.RWMutex): Get costs ~50ns, Set costs ~100ns — fastest option, limited to single process memory.
Distributed cache (Redis): Get costs ~1ms (network round-trip), Set costs ~1ms — 20x slower than in-memory but shared across service instances.

Cache hit ratio determines effectiveness: 90% hit ratio means 90% of requests served from cache (1ms), 10% hit the DB (50ms). Effective latency = 0.9 × 1ms + 0.1 × 50ms = 5.9ms — 8x faster than 100% DB hits (50ms).
Cache memory: 1000 entries at 1KB each = 1MB — negligible. 10M entries at 1KB each = 10GB — significant. Use TTL and eviction policies (LRU, LFU, TTL) to bound memory usage.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go developers query the database on every request, even for data that rarely changes (product catalog, user profiles, configuration). This increases database load, request latency, and infrastructure costs. Caching stores frequently-read data in fast memory, reducing database queries by 90%+ and improving response times from 50ms to 1ms.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement a caching layer for a product catalog service with: (1) cache-aside pattern using Redis (simulated with a Go map + mutex), (2) TTL with jitter, (3) cache stampede prevention using singleflight, (4) write-through cache invalidation, (5) a benchmark that measures latency with and without cache, (6) a test that proves the cache reduces database load by 90%.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Caching is the most common performance optimization in production Go services. Redis is the standard distributed cache, used by: Twitter (user timelines cached in Redis), GitHub (repository data cached in Redis), Stack Overflow (page content cached in Redis). In-memory caching (sync.Map or go-cache) is used for service-local data that does not need cross-instance consistency: feature flags, configuration, rate limit counters.
step_by_step_execution: Identify cache targets: endpoints with high read-to-write ratio (product catalog 100:1, user profiles 50:1)
Choose cache type: in-memory for single-instance services, Redis for multi-instance or shared cache
Implement cache-aside: cache.Get(key) → if miss { data := db.Query(key); cache.Set(key, data, TTL); return data }
Add stampede protection: wrap cache miss with singleflight.Group — only one goroutine hits the DB per key
Add invalidation: on every write, cache.Delete(key) — ensures the next read fetches fresh data
Monitor cache hit ratio: metric cache_hit_total / (cache_hit_total + cache_miss_total) — target > 90%
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go's singleflight.Group (golang.org/x/sync/singleflight) coalesces concurrent function calls with the same key. When 100 goroutines call g.Do(

, fn), only one executes fn, and all 100 receive the same result. This is the canonical Go solution for cache stampede prevention. The implementation: singleflight uses a sync.Mutex + map[string]*call. The first call creates a *call entry with a channel. Subsequent calls find the existing entry and wait on the channel. When the first call completes, it sends the result on the channel, waking all waiters. The entry is then deleted from the map.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Caching exists because databases are slow compared to memory. A database query takes 10-100ms (network + disk I/O + query execution). A memory read takes 50-100ns. Caching bridges this gap: frequently accessed data is served from memory (nanoseconds) instead of the database (milliseconds). Without caching, a 1000 req/s service generates 1000 DB queries/second. With caching at 90% hit rate, the same service generates 100 DB queries/second — a 10x reduction in database load that translates directly to lower latency, lower cost, and higher throughput.
