items.title: When to split services
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply When to split services in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Splitting a service because a single binary feels 'unclean' — this is the most common mistake. A monolith that is well-structured (modular monolith with domain packages) handles 98% of use cases. Splitting adds: network latency (1-10ms per call), distributed tracing, data consistency challenges, deployment coordination, and operational complexity. Only split when there is a concrete, measured need.

Splitting by layer instead of domain — creating separate services for 'handlers', 'services', and 'repositories'. Each service is tightly coupled to the others (handlers need services, services need repositories). The split increases complexity without any of the benefits of independence. Split by domain boundary: UserService, OrderService, PaymentService — each owns its full stack.

Splitting before the data access pattern is understood — a monolith makes JOINs and transactions easy. After splitting, the same queries require API calls between services or an API composition layer. If the team does not know which queries need to JOIN across domains, they will create a chatty, high-latency architecture.
execution_timeline: Team has a monolith with 3 domains: catalog, orders, payments — well-structured as a modular monolith
→ Team grows from 5 to 20 engineers: deployment process takes 30 minutes because all 20 engineers push to the same repo
→ Team measures: deployment frequency is 2/week, the bottleneck is the deployment pipeline (all 20 engineers waiting for CI on the same repo)
→ Team decides to split: extract the payments domain into a separate service (Payments API)
→ Payments service has its own repo, CI, deployment, and database — team A owns it
→ Catalog and orders stay as a modular monolith — team B owns them
→ Result: team A deploys 10x/week (autonomous), team B deploys 2x/week (still blocked by their internal coordination)
→ Team B splits further: extract orders into its own service when the team grows to 15 engineers
→ Each split is justified by a measured bottleneck, not by aesthetics
failure_modes: Chatty service communication: service A calls B (5ms), B calls C (5ms), C calls D (10ms) — each request takes 20ms just in network overhead. If A needs 10 calls to B, each request is 50ms+ in serial network calls. Fix: use bulk APIs, cache responses, or merge services.

Distributed transaction complexity: creating an order requires decrementing inventory in the Inventory service AND creating an invoice in the Billing service. A monolith does this in one transaction. After splitting, it requires a saga or two-phase commit — significantly more complex.

Data inconsistency due to stale caches: the Catalog service caches product data. The Inventory service updates product availability. The Catalog cache serves stale availability data for 5 minutes. Fix: event-driven cache invalidation (Inventory publishes 'product.availability_changed', Catalog invalidates cache).
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must list 3 concrete, measurable signals that a monolith should be split (deployment time > 30 min, team coordination overhead > 20% of time, database connection pool exhaustion) and explain how each signal is measured.

Learner must design the service boundary for a given system (e.g., a video streaming platform) and justify why each boundary was chosen based on team structure, data access patterns, and deployment frequency.

Learner must identify the wrong reason to split (aesthetic preference, resume-driven development, 'microservices are the standard') vs. the right reason (independent deployability, team autonomy, independent scaling).
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go monoliths are split into services by extracting packages into separate binaries. The package boundary that already exists in the modular monolith becomes the service boundary. The Go package's exported interface becomes the service's gRPC/HTTP API. The internal/ convention becomes the service boundary — one module's internal/ is the service's private code. The dependency graph between packages maps to the service dependency graph between services.
zero_magic.mental_model: Splitting a monolith is like moving from a shared house to separate apartments. The shared house (monolith) has one kitchen, one bathroom, one front door — everything is shared, coordination is simple, but everyone must agree on everything. Separate apartments (services) have their own kitchen, bathroom, and door — each team can paint their walls any color, but now they need to visit each other (network calls) to borrow things. The key question: when does the cost of visiting (network calls, data duplication, deployment complexity) become less than the cost of coordination in the shared house?
performance_implications: Service split adds: 1-10ms latency per inter-service call, serialization/deserialization overhead (1-10µs), connection pool management for each downstream service.
Data duplication: each service may need its own copy of shared data (e.g., both Orders and Payments need user data). This increases storage and adds synchronization complexity.
The counterintuitive insight: splitting a service often INCREASES total CPU and memory because of: duplicate data, network serialization, and health check/observability overhead per service.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go teams split their monolith into microservices because it is the 'industry standard,' without understanding the tradeoffs. They create distributed monoliths (services that are tightly coupled by chatty API calls) with all the complexity of microservices and none of the benefits. This lesson teaches the concrete signals that justify a split: deployment frequency bottlenecks, team coordination overhead, independent scaling needs, and technology heterogeneity requirements.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner is given a modular monolith with 4 domains (inventory, orders, shipping, notifications) and must: (1) identify which domain(s) are candidates for extraction based on team size (10 engineers per domain), deployment frequency (2/week for monolith, target 10/week), and scaling requirements (inventory needs 5x more capacity during sales), (2) design the extraction plan (which domain first, what API, how to handle shared data), (3) justify why the remaining domains stay as a monolith.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: The standard advice for software architecture is 'start with a monolith, extract services when you have a proven need.' Amazon ran a monolith for years before splitting. eBay ran a monolith for a decade. Etsy still runs a monolith. Shopify extracted its first service after 8 years. The key insight: premature splitting creates a distributed monolith with all the complexity and none of the benefits. The right time to split is when the cost of coordination in the monolith exceeds the cost of network calls between services.
step_by_step_execution: Measure the bottleneck: deployment frequency, team coordination time, database connection count, build time
Identify the domain causing the bottleneck: is it catalog (infrequently changed), orders (frequently changed), or payments (needs PCI compliance)?
Design the extracted service's API: the interface that the monolith already uses for that domain becomes the REST/gRPC API
Extract the code: copy the package to a new repo, replace in-process calls with network calls
Set up the new service: CI/CD, database, monitoring, deployment
Migrate one consumer at a time: start with a non-critical path, verify correctness, then migrate all traffic
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Service extraction in Go follows a mechanical process: (1) identify the package to extract — it already has a well-defined exported interface (the Go package API). (2) Create a new Go module (go mod init service-name). (3) Copy the package and its dependencies into the new module. (4) Add a server that exposes the package's exported methods over gRPC or HTTP. (5) In the original monolith, replace the direct package call with a gRPC/HTTP client call. (6) Add observability (tracing, metrics, logging) to the network boundary. (7) Verify that the behavior is identical — run integration tests that cover both in-process and network paths.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Service splitting exists because monoliths have limits: at a certain scale (100+ engineers, 1M+ LOC), the deployment pipeline becomes a bottleneck, the database connection pool is exhausted, and team coordination costs exceed the cost of network calls. Splitting at the right time (measured by these signals, not by aesthetics) gives independent deployability, team autonomy, and independent scaling. Splitting at the wrong time (because 'microservices are the standard') adds complexity without solving any concrete problem.

items.title: Microservices as a tradeoff, not a default
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Microservices as a tradeoff, not a default in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Adopting microservices because 'everyone does it' — this is the most expensive architectural mistake a team can make. Microservices solve specific problems: independent deployability, team autonomy, polyglot persistence, and independent scaling. If the team does not have these problems, microservices add complexity without benefit.

Believing microservices are 'faster' — development speed DECREASES initially because of: network error handling, distributed tracing, data consistency, deployment pipelines, service discovery, and API versioning. Speed increases only after the team masters the operational complexity, which takes 6-18 months.

Using microservices to fix a team problem — a team is slow because of unclear ownership, poor communication, or lack of testing. They split into microservices hoping the architecture will fix the team problem. It does not: the same team problems manifest as integration problems between services, but now debugging is harder.
execution_timeline: Team of 8 builds a monolith for 18 months — stable, fast, well-structured
→ Company grows: team splits into 3 squads (catalog, orders, payments) — 8 engineers each
→ Monolith is a bottleneck: all 3 squads must coordinate on every deployment → deployment frequency drops to 1/week
→ Team measures: 30% of sprint time is spent on deployment coordination
→ Team extracts payments into a separate service — the squad that needs independent deployability gets it
→ Payments squad: deploys 10x/week, fine. Catalog + orders: still deploy as a monolith, 2x/week
→ 6 months later: catalog squad also extracts — now all 3 services deploy independently
→ Total deployment frequency: 22x/week (10+7+5), up from 1x/week — worth the complexity
failure_modes: Distributed monolith: services are tightly coupled by synchronous API calls that must all succeed for a request to complete. Each call adds latency, and a failure in any service fails the request. Fix: use async communication (events, queues) for non-critical paths, and keep synchronous calls only for paths where consistency is required.

Shared database between services: services share a database (or even a table) because 'it is faster than setting up an API.' Now changing a table schema requires coordinated deployment across services. Fix: each service owns its data; access through the service API only.

Orchestration complexity: microservices require service discovery, API gateways, circuit breakers, distributed tracing, centralized logging, configuration management, and secret management. Each component adds operational overhead. A team of 8 may spend 50% of their time maintaining infrastructure instead of building product features.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must list the concrete costs of microservices (network latency, distributed tracing, data consistency, deployment complexity, debugging difficulty, operational overhead) and explain how each cost manifests in a Go service.

Learner must evaluate a given scenario (e.g., a 5-person startup building a MVP, a 50-person company with 3 product teams, a 500-person company with compliance requirements) and recommend monolith vs. microservices with justification.
Learner must explain why Conway's Law (organizations design systems that mirror their communication structure) means microservices should follow team boundaries, not technical layers.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's philosophy (simplicity, explicit errors, fast compilation) aligns with monolithic architecture. A Go monolith compiles in seconds, deploys as a single binary, and has no network overhead for internal calls. When teams do adopt microservices in Go, they use: gRPC for inter-service communication (protobuf for type safety, HTTP/2 for performance), Prometheus for metrics, OpenTelemetry for distributed tracing, and Kubernetes for deployment. Go's compilation speed (seconds per service) makes microservices deployment practical — a monolith that takes 30 minutes to compile is a stronger reason to split.
zero_magic.mental_model: Microservices are a loan, not an investment. They give you immediate benefits (independent deployability, team autonomy) but the interest payments (operational complexity, debugging difficulty, latency, data inconsistency) are high and compound over time. The loan is worth it only if the benefits exceed the interest payments. Most teams take the loan and then are surprised by the interest. Before adopting microservices, calculate the interest: how much time will the team spend on CI/CD, observability, service discovery, and debugging network failures?
performance_implications: Network call (gRPC between services): 1-5ms vs. in-process call: ~1ns. A request that makes 5 internal calls goes from 5ns to 5-25ms — 1,000,000x slower.
Serialization/deserialization: protobuf adds ~1-10µs per message. JSON adds ~10-100µs. For 10 services in a request path, this is 10-1000µs of overhead.
Container overhead: each Go service runs in a container with ~50MB base memory. 10 services = 500MB just for the OS + runtime. A monolith runs in one container: ~50MB.
The performance of Go microservices is dominated by network, not compute. Optimize by reducing inter-service calls, using batch APIs, and caching aggressively.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go teams adopt microservices as the default architecture, assuming they are 'modern' and 'scalable.' They do not understand the tradeoffs: microservices increase latency, operational complexity, and debugging difficulty. This lesson presents the concrete costs and benefits, enabling the learner to make an informed decision. The recommendation: start with a modular monolith, extract services only when there is a measured need (deployment bottleneck, team autonomy, independent scaling).
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a decision analysis document for a given system (e.g., a 10-person startup building a food delivery platform) that: (1) lists the concrete costs of microservices for this specific system (operational complexity, latency, debugging difficulty), (2) lists the concrete benefits that would justify a split (which must be measured, not hypothetical), (3) recommends monolith vs. microservices with a timeline for when to reconsider (e.g., 'Start with monolith, consider splitting payments when we have 3+ engineers per domain and deployment frequency drops below 5/week').
zero_magic.real_world_usage: The industry has largely rejected the 'microservices first' approach. Amazon's CTO Werner Vogels: 'It is much easier to make a monolith into microservices than to start with microservices.' Martin Fowler (ThoughtWorks): 'First make the monolith modular, then extract services only where needed.' Kelsey Hightower (Google): 'Start with a monolith. Microservices are a last resort.' The pendulum has swung back: modular monoliths are the recommended starting point for most teams. Microservices are a tool for specific problems, not a default architecture.
step_by_step_execution: Measure current bottlenecks: deployment frequency, build time, database connections, team coordination overhead
List the problems that microservices would solve: independent deployability, team autonomy, independent scaling, technology heterogeneity
List the costs of microservices for THIS system: operational complexity, latency, debugging difficulty, data consistency
Compare: do the problems being solved justify the costs? If not, stay with a modular monolith
If yes: extract ONE service at a time (the one solving the biggest bottleneck), not all at once
After extraction: measure the improvement. Did deployment frequency increase? Did team coordination decrease? If not, the extraction was premature.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go's toolchain makes microservices practical: each service is a separate Go module with its own go.mod. Compilation takes seconds. Container images are small (scratch-based: 5-15MB per binary). The go tool does not enforce any architecture — a monolith and a set of microservices compile the same way. The difference is in the deployment topology: monolith = one binary, one deployment; microservices = many binaries, many deployments. Go's fast compilation means the build pipeline is not the bottleneck — the operational complexity of coordinating many deployments is the bottleneck.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Microservices exist as an option, not a default, because they solve specific problems that monoliths cannot: (1) independent deployability — team A deploys without coordinating with team B; (2) independent scaling — the payments service scales to 100 instances while the catalog service stays at 5; (3) technology heterogeneity — the payments service uses PostgreSQL, the catalog service uses MongoDB; (4) failure isolation — a crash in the payments service does not bring down the catalog service. If the team does not have these problems, a monolith is simpler and faster.

items.title: Architecture decision records
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Architecture decision records in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Writing ADRs as design documents (10-page Word docs that no one reads) — ADRs must be short: title, status, context, decision, consequences. If it takes more than 5 minutes to write, the team will not write them. A good ADR is 5-10 sentences.

Writing ADRs after the decision is implemented — by the time the code is written, the decision is already made and the context is lost. ADRs must be written BEFORE or DURING the decision process, capturing the alternatives considered and the reasoning.

Not updating ADR status when the decision changes — the team decides to use PostgreSQL, writes ADR-001. Six months later they switch to CockroachDB, but ADR-001 still says 'status: accepted' and no one knows why they switched. ADRs must be living documents: superseded ADRs get status 'deprecated' and reference the new ADR.
execution_timeline: Team needs to choose a database for a new service → discussion involves PostgreSQL, CockroachDB, and DynamoDB
→ Engineer creates ADR-001: 'Database selection for Order Service' with status 'proposed'
→ Team discusses tradeoffs: PostgreSQL (familiar, SQL, ACID) vs. CockroachDB (horizontal scaling, PostgreSQL-compatible) vs. DynamoDB (managed, NoSQL, single-digit ms latency)
→ Team agrees: PostgreSQL for the initial version, CockroachDB if we need horizontal scaling later
→ ADR-001 updated: status 'accepted', decision 'PostgreSQL', alternatives listed with reasons for not choosing each
→ 6 months later: Order Service needs to scale horizontally for a new product launch
→ Engineer creates ADR-002: 'Migrate to CockroachDB for horizontal scaling' with status 'proposed'
→ Team reviews: ADR-001's context section already documented why CockroachDB was considered but not chosen initially — now the scaling requirement changes the tradeoff
→ Team decides to migrate → ADR-002 status 'accepted', ADR-001 status 'deprecated' with link to ADR-002
→ New engineer joins → reads ADR-001 and ADR-002 → understands why the team chose PostgreSQL and then migrated to CockroachDB — 10 minutes of reading instead of 3 meetings
failure_modes: ADR not reviewed — a team member writes an ADR and no one reads it. The decision is made by the author alone, and the team does not buy into it. Fix: ADRs must be reviewed in a team meeting (max 15 minutes per ADR).

ADR too detailed — 5 pages of analysis for choosing between Redis and Memcached. The detail is not helpful and discourages future ADR writing. Fix: ADRs should be 1-2 paragraphs per section. If more detail is needed, link to a separate design document.

ADR not used as a reference — the team writes ADRs but never reads them. When a new engineer asks why PostgreSQL was chosen, the team answers 'I don't know, it was before my time.' Fix: reference ADRs in code comments and README files: // See ADR-001: Database selection for Order Service.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must write an ADR for a given architectural decision (e.g., choosing between gRPC and REST for inter-service communication) with all required sections: title, status, context, decision, consequences.
Learner must review an existing ADR and identify the missing sections (e.g., ADR that says 'we chose Redis' but does not list alternatives or consequences).

Learner must explain why ADRs are stored in the repository alongside the code rather than in a wiki — ADRs in the repo are versioned alongside the code, so every commit is associated with the relevant ADRs.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go teams store ADRs in the repository as Markdown files: docs/adr/ADR-001-database-selection.md. The filename includes the ADR number and a short title. ADRs are version-controlled alongside the code, so git blame shows who made each decision and when. Tooling: adr-tools (npm) provides CLI for creating and managing ADRs. Go teams integrate ADR review into the PR review process — an ADR PR is reviewed before the implementation PR.
zero_magic.mental_model: ADRs are commit messages for architecture decisions. A commit message explains WHY a code change was made. An ADR explains WHY an architecture decision was made. Without ADRs, the team has to ask 'why did we choose PostgreSQL?' and the answer is 'I don't know, it was before my time.' With ADRs, the answer is in the repo: read ADR-001. ADRs preserve context that would otherwise be lost when team members leave or memories fade.
performance_implications: Time cost: writing an ADR takes 15-30 minutes. Reviewing an ADR takes 5-10 minutes per reviewer. For a team of 5 making 1 ADR per sprint (2 weeks), the cost is ~2 hours per sprint — negligible.
Time saved: a new engineer can read 10 ADRs in 1 hour and understand the architecture rationale. Without ADRs, understanding the same decisions requires 5-10 hours of meetings and code archaeology.
ROI: ADRs pay for themselves the first time a new engineer joins and reads them instead of scheduling 5 meetings to understand past decisions.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go teams make architecture decisions in meetings or Slack threads. Six months later, no one remembers why a decision was made. When a new decision contradicts an old one, the team repeats the same discussion because the original context is lost. ADRs document decisions in a structured, version-controlled format that the whole team can reference. They preserve context, list alternatives considered, and track the evolution of architecture decisions over time.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write 3 ADRs for a Go service architecture: (1) ADR-001: choosing between go-chi and standard net/http for HTTP routing, (2) ADR-002: choosing between direct SQL and an ORM (sqlx vs. GORM) for database access, (3) ADR-003: choosing between in-process caching and Redis for session storage. Each ADR must have: title, status (proposed), context (the problem being solved), decision (the chosen option), consequences (what becomes easier/harder), and alternatives (at least 2 alternatives with reasons for rejection).
zero_magic.real_world_usage: ADRs are standard practice in every mature engineering organization. ThoughtWorks pioneered the format and uses it in all client engagements. The Kubernetes project uses ADRs (called KEPs — Kubernetes Enhancement Proposals). The Go project itself uses proposal documents that follow the ADR format. ADRs are stored in the repository at docs/adr/ or docs/decisions/. The format is standardized: title, status, context, decision, consequences, compliance, notes.
step_by_step_execution: Identify a decision that needs to be made: e.g., choosing between Redis and in-memory cache
Create ADR file: docs/adr/ADR-004-cache-strategy.md
Write title: 'Cache strategy for User Profile Service'
Write context: 'User profiles are read 100x more than written. Current implementation queries PostgreSQL on every read. Target: 1ms P99 latency.'
Write decision: 'Use Redis with cache-aside pattern, 5-minute TTL, and singleflight for stampede protection'
Write consequences: 'Pros: 1ms P99 read latency, reduces PostgreSQL load by 90%. Cons: adds Redis operational dependency, stale data possible for up to 5 minutes.'

Write alternatives: 'Option A (in-memory Go map): simpler, no network dependency, but cache is per-instance and lost on restart. Option B (no cache): simpler, always consistent, but 50ms P99 latency.'
Set status: 'proposed' — team reviews in the next architecture meeting
After review: update status to 'accepted' or 'rejected' with reason
zero_magic.under_the_hood: ADRs are plain Markdown files — no special tooling required. The standard format (Michael Nygard's template) has 5 sections: Title (ADR-XXX: Decision title), Status (Proposed, Accepted, Deprecated, Superseded), Context (why this decision is needed, what problem it solves, what constraints exist), Decision (the chosen option, with justification), Consequences (what becomes easier and harder as a result). ADRs are stored in a docs/adr/ directory in the repository. The filename convention is NNNN-title-with-hyphens.md. The first ADR (ADR-001) explains the ADR format itself. Tools like adr-tools and adr-log automate ADR management, but a text editor and git are sufficient.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: ADRs exist because institutional knowledge decays exponentially with time. A decision made in a meeting is forgotten in weeks. A decision documented in a Slack thread is lost when the workspace is archived. A decision in a wiki is versionless — no one knows when it was updated or why. An ADR in the repository is: versioned alongside the code, searchable by git grep, reviewable via PR, and attributable by git blame. ADRs ensure that architecture decisions are preserved, reviewable, and actionable — not lost to attrition.

items.title: Linux processes
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Linux processes in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using exec.Command without checking the process exit code — a command that fails (exit code 1) still returns no error from Run/Wait if the process ran. The error is only returned when the process exits non-zero with an ExitError that contains stderr. Always check the error from cmd.Wait() and the exit code.

Zombie processes from not calling cmd.Wait() — creating a process with cmd.Start() and forgetting to call cmd.Wait() leaves the child process as a zombie (defunct) until the parent exits. The OS keeps the process table entry until Wait is called to collect the exit status.

Not handling SIGCHLD in long-running Go services — when a child process exits, the kernel sends SIGCHLD to the parent. If the parent is a Go program that does not handle SIGCHLD, the child becomes a zombie. Go's os/exec handles this internally for managed processes, but processes created via syscall.ForkExec directly can leak zombies.

Assuming os/exec.FindProcess finds a running process — FindProcess on Unix returns a Process struct even if the PID does not exist. It only checks that the PID is valid syntactically (PID > 0). The process may have already exited. Always use signal or /proc to verify the process is alive.
execution_timeline: Go program calls exec.Command("sleep", "10") → cmd.Start() spawns a child process
→ Kernel creates a new process with a new PID, copies the parent's address space (copy-on-write)
→ Child process exec()s /bin/sleep with the given arguments
→ Parent Go goroutine continues executing while the child runs in the background
→ Parent calls cmd.Wait() → parent blocks until the child exits
→ Child process exits (sleep completes) → kernel sends SIGCHLD to parent
→ cmd.Wait() returns the ExitError (if non-zero exit) or nil (exit code 0)
→ Process table entry is cleaned up: zombie is reaped
failure_modes: Zombie process accumulation — a service that spawns many short-lived child processes without calling Wait on each one accumulates zombies in the process table. Each zombie consumes a PID and kernel memory. When the PID limit is reached (/proc/sys/kernel/pid_max), the OS cannot create new processes. Fix: always call cmd.Wait() in a deferred function or use a goroutine to wait.

Process group orphaned — a parent process exits before its children, and the children are not in a different process group. The children become orphans and receive SIGHUP (if they have no controlling terminal). With SIGHUP defaulting to terminate, the children are killed. Fix: use cmd.SysProcAttr.Setpgid = true to put children in their own process group.

Reading stdout/stderr before Wait — reading from cmd.Stdout before calling cmd.Wait() can deadlock if the pipe buffer fills up (64KB on Linux). The child process blocks writing to stdout, the parent blocks reading from stdout, and neither can proceed. Fix: use cmd.StdoutPipe() and read in a goroutine, or use cmd.Output() which handles this internally.

Exec a binary that does not exist — exec.Command("nonexistent") does not fail until Start()/Run() is called. The error from Start() is exec.ErrNotFound wrapped in an ExitError. The error message includes the binary name and PATH search result.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must write a Go program that spawns a child process, reads its stdout line by line, waits for it to complete, and checks the exit code.
Learner must identify why a Go service has 1000 zombie processes and write the fix (ensure cmd.Wait() is called for every cmd.Start()).
Learner must write a program that runs a command that may not exist and handles the error correctly (checking exit code vs. command-not-found).
Learner must explain why os/exec.FindProcess does not work for checking if a process is alive and write an alternative using /proc or signal(0).
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's os/exec package wraps syscall.ForkExec for spawning external processes. It handles stdout/stderr piping, stdin feeding, environment variable propagation, and exit code collection. Standard library tools like go build, go test, and go vet use exec.Command internally to invoke the compiler and linker. Go's test runner spawns test binaries as child processes. Go's os.StartProcess is the lower-level API that exec.Command builds on, managing file descriptors, process groups, and signals.
zero_magic.mental_model: A process is an instance of a running program with its own address space, file descriptors, and process ID. In Go, exec.Command creates a child process that is isolated from the parent — changes to the child do not affect the parent. The parent-child relationship is asymmetric: the parent can wait for the child (Wait), send it signals (Signal), or kill it (Kill), but the child cannot affect the parent. Go's goroutines are lightweight threads within the same process, while processes are OS-level heavyweight isolation units.
performance_implications: Creating a process is expensive: ~10ms per fork+exec on Linux, plus memory copy (COW). For high-throughput systems, process creation should be avoided in the hot path.
Pipe overhead: each pipe between parent and child (stdout, stderr, stdin) adds ~4KB kernel buffer per pipe. Reading pipes in Go adds goroutine scheduling overhead.
Process table limits: each process consumes an entry in the kernel's process table. Default max is 32768 PIDs on 64-bit Linux. Exceeding this requires root to adjust /proc/sys/kernel/pid_max.
Go's syscall.Rusage provides process resource usage statistics (CPU time, memory, I/O) after Wait(). This is useful for measuring subprocess resource consumption.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go developers treat exec.Command as a black box — they run commands without checking exit codes, forget to wait for completion, or create zombie processes. This lesson covers the full lifecycle of a child process in Go: spawning, piping I/O, waiting, exit codes, and cleanup. The learner understands that a process is a resource that must be managed: created, monitored, and reaped.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a Go program that: (1) spawns 5 child processes running 'sleep 1' in parallel using exec.Command, (2) collects their stdout/stderr, (3) waits for all to complete, (4) checks each exit code, (5) reports the total wall time and average exit code. They must then modify the program to handle the case where one of the commands does not exist (exits with exec.ErrNotFound).
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Go's build system uses process spawning extensively: go build invokes the Go compiler as a subprocess, go test compiles and runs test binaries as subprocesses, and go generate runs user-specified commands as subprocesses. Production Go services use exec.Command for: running backup scripts, invoking git for CI/CD workflows, calling ffmpeg for video processing pipelines, running database migrations as subprocesses, and executing shell commands in admin APIs.
step_by_step_execution: Define: cmd := exec.Command("ls", "-la", "/tmp") — creates a Cmd struct, does not start the process
Set up pipes: cmd.Stdout = &bytes.Buffer{} or use cmd.StdoutPipe() for streaming
Start: cmd.Start() — fork+exec the child process, returns immediately
Optional: cmd.Process.Signal(syscall.SIGTERM) — send a signal to the child
Wait: err := cmd.Wait() — blocks until the child exits, returns ExitError if exit code != 0
Read output: stdout.String() or read from the pipe — the full output is available after Wait
Check exit code: exitCode := cmd.ProcessState.ExitCode() — 0 is success, non-zero is failure
Clean up: defer cmd.Wait() if using cmd.Start() directly — prevents zombie processes
zero_magic.under_the_hood: exec.Command creates the Cmd struct but does not fork. Calling Start() invokes os.StartProcess, which calls syscall.ForkExec on Unix. ForkExec clones the current process (fork), replaces the child's memory with the target binary (execve), and returns the child's PID. The child inherits the parent's file descriptors unless cmd.ExtraFiles or cmd.Stdout/Stderr/Stdin are set — in which case Go creates pipes (socketpair on Linux) between parent and child. cmd.Wait() calls syscall.Wait4(pid, &wstatus, 0, &rusage) which blocks until the child changes state (exits, is signaled, or is stopped). The wstatus encodes the exit code and signal information. Go's ProcessState parses wstatus to provide ExitCode(), Success(), and String().
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Linux processes exist because the OS needs to isolate running programs. Each process has its own virtual address space, file descriptors, and security context, preventing one program from corrupting another. In Go, exec.Command provides a safe, cross-platform API for spawning and managing child processes, handling the OS-specific syscall details behind a unified interface.

items.title: Signals
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Signals in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using an unbuffered channel for signal.Notify — when a signal arrives and the channel is not ready to receive (main goroutine is busy), the signal is dropped. This causes the process to miss SIGTERM and not shut down. Always use a buffered channel of size 1 for signal.Notify.

Calling signal.Notify without removing the default handler — signal.Notify adds the channel to the signal's list of recipients but does NOT replace the OS default action. For SIGINT and SIGTERM, the default action is to terminate the process. If the channel is not read in time, the default handler still runs and kills the process. Fix: signal.Reset() or signal.Ignore() for signals you want to handle yourself.

Forgetting that signal.Notify affects all goroutines — calling signal.Notify in one goroutine registers the channel globally. Any signal sent to the process is delivered to that channel, even if another goroutine expected to handle it. Signal handlers are process-wide, not goroutine-scoped.

Ignoring SIGPIPE — writing to a broken pipe (e.g., writing to stdout when the reader has exited) sends SIGPIPE to the writing process with default action 'terminate'. Go's os/signal package does not intercept SIGPIPE by default, so a Go program writing to a closed stdout is killed silently. Fix: signal.Notify(sigCh, syscall.SIGPIPE) and handle it or ignore it.
execution_timeline: Signal generated: kernel, user (Ctrl+C), or another process sends a signal (e.g., SIGTERM)
→ Kernel checks the process's signal disposition table: default, ignore, or handler registered
→ If handler registered (signal.Notify): kernel saves the signal in the pending signal set
→ When the process resumes execution (from kernel to user space): kernel delivers pending signals
→ Signal delivery: kernel pushes a signal frame on the process's stack, saves the execution context
→ Go runtime's sigtramp handles the signal: finds the registered channel from the global signal map
→ Go runtime sends the signal to the channel: sigCh <- syscall.SIGTERM (non-blocking on buffered channel)
→ Go goroutine (main or dedicated signal handler) receives: <-sigCh
→ Handler goroutine executes: cancel(), server.Shutdown(ctx), wg.Wait(), os.Exit(0)
failure_modes: Signal dropped: channel is unbuffered, main goroutine is handling a request — SIGTERM arrives, channel send blocks, kernel moves on, signal is never delivered. Process keeps running until Kubernetes sends SIGKILL (10 seconds later), causing hard shutdown and request failures.

Signal race: SIGTERM and SIGINT sent in rapid succession — first signal triggers shutdown, second signal is ignored (channel already has one). This is fine for idempotent shutdown, but if a second SIGTERM is meant to force-kill after a grace period, the process never receives it.

SIGKILL cannot be caught: signal.Notify ignores SIGKILL and SIGSTOP — they are uncatchable by design. A process that must shut down within a deadline must handle SIGTERM; SIGKILL is the final timeout.
SIGCHLD not handled: Go services that spawn child processes (exec.Command) must handle SIGCHLD or use cmd.Wait(). Unhandled SIGCHLD causes zombie processes to accumulate.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must write a Go program that handles SIGINT and SIGTERM by printing a message and exiting cleanly.
Learner must identify why a Go program sometimes misses Ctrl+C (unbuffered channel) and fix it.
Learner must explain why SIGKILL cannot be caught and what that means for shutdown design.
Learner must write a program that registers multiple signal handlers for different signals (SIGUSR1 for reload, SIGTERM for shutdown).
Learner must test their signal handler by sending signals to the running process from another terminal.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's os/signal package provides signal.Notify(ch chan<- os.Signal, sig ...os.Signal) to register a channel for signal delivery. signal.NotifyContext(ctx, sig...) creates a context that is canceled when any of the specified signals arrive. Go's standard library uses signals internally: the HTTP server uses SIGPIPE handling to avoid crashing when writing to closed connections, the runtime uses SIGURG for preemptive goroutine scheduling, and SIGPROF for CPU profiling.
zero_magic.mental_model: Signals are OS-level interrupts delivered to a process. Think of them as hardware interrupts for software — they preempt normal execution to notify the process of external events. A signal handler is like an interrupt service routine (ISR): it must be fast, non-blocking, and reentrant. Go's signal.Notify converts OS signals into Go channel messages, allowing goroutines to handle signals synchronously instead of in an interrupt context.
performance_implications: Signal delivery latency: from kernel signal generation to Go channel receive, the latency is typically 1-10 microseconds. This is fast enough for shutdown handling but too slow for high-frequency signaling.

Signal channel overhead: each signal.Notify registration adds a channel to the global signal map. With many registrations, signal delivery becomes O(n) where n is the number of channels registered for that signal.
signal.NotifyContext overhead: spawns a goroutine that blocks on the signal channel. For most programs, one signal goroutine is negligible.

Real-time signals (SIGRTMIN+): Linux supports 32 real-time signals (SIGRTMIN to SIGRTMAX) with guaranteed delivery ordering — older signals are delivered before newer ones of the same type. Go's os/signal supports them, but they are rarely used.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go developers deploy services to Kubernetes without signal handling. When Kubernetes sends SIGTERM during rolling updates or node drains, the process exits immediately (default SIGTERM action is terminate), cutting in-flight requests and corrupting state. This lesson covers how to catch signals (SIGINT, SIGTERM, SIGHUP), what each signal means, how to avoid buffered-channel signal loss, and how to build a graceful shutdown sequence.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a Go program that: (1) starts an HTTP server on :8080, (2) registers signal handlers for SIGINT and SIGTERM, (3) on signal, performs a graceful shutdown (server.Shutdown with 5-second timeout), (4) prints a shutdown log message, (5) exits with code 0. They must write a test that sends SIGTERM to the process and verifies the server shuts down cleanly.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every Go service in Kubernetes relies on signal handling for graceful shutdown. Common patterns: SIGTERM triggers graceful shutdown (HTTP drain, worker drain, DB close), SIGHUP triggers config reload (hot-reload without restart), SIGUSR1 triggers log rotation (reopen log files after rotation), SIGUSR2 triggers debug endpoints (pprof, goroutine dump). Opslane's graceful shutdown handles SIGTERM by draining HTTP connections, NACKing queue messages, flushing metrics, and closing the database pool.
step_by_step_execution: Build: create signal channel: sigCh := make(chan os.Signal, 1)
Build: register signals: signal.Notify(sigCh, syscall.SIGINT, syscall.SIGTERM)
Build: create a context for shutdown: ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 30*time.Second)
Build: start a goroutine that blocks on sigCh: go func() { <-sigCh; cancel() }()
Build: or use signal.NotifyContext: ctx, stop := signal.NotifyContext(context.Background(), syscall.SIGINT, syscall.SIGTERM)
Build: create HTTP server and start it
Build: <-ctx.Done() blocks the main goroutine until signal or timeout
Build: when context is done (signal received): server.Shutdown(context.Background())
Build: after shutdown: log 'server stopped', call stop(), close resources, os.Exit(0)
Test: run the program, send Ctrl+C, observe graceful shutdown
Test: run in Kubernetes, trigger rolling update, observe zero-downtime shutdown
zero_magic.under_the_hood: signal.Notify registers a channel in a global map of signal->channels. When a signal arrives, the Go runtime's signal handler (installed in sigtramp) looks up the registered channels in the map and sends the signal to each channel in a non-blocking select. If the channel is full (unbuffered or buffer full), the signal is dropped — the send is skipped. signal.NotifyContext creates a cancel context and spawns a goroutine that listens on the signal channel, calling cancel() when a signal arrives. The context's cancel function deregisters the signal handler to prevent goroutine leaks. Under the hood, Go replaces the OS signal handler with its own sigtramp handler that queues signals in a global signal ring buffer and wakes up the runtime's signal goroutine via eventfd (Linux) or mach port (macOS).
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Signals exist because the OS needs to communicate with running processes asynchronously. Without signals, a process would have to poll for external events, wasting CPU and adding latency. Signals provide a lightweight, interrupt-based mechanism for the kernel to notify processes about: termination requests (SIGTERM), user interrupts (SIGINT), process death (SIGCHLD), pipe breaks (SIGPIPE), and timer expiration (SIGALRM). Go's signal.Notify bridges OS signals with Go's channel model, allowing goroutines to handle signals synchronously without blocking the OS signal handler.

items.title: Logs
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Logs in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Logging to files instead of stdout: Beginners write logs to /var/log/app.log, violating the 12-factor app principle. In Docker, container stdout/stderr is captured by the runtime; writing to files fills the container layer and breaks log aggregation.

Using log.Println for everything: New Go devs use log.Println at every execution point without log levels. Production systems need DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR, FATAL levels so operators can filter noise and route errors to alerting pipelines.

Calling log.Fatal in library code: Learners call log.Fatal(err) inside packages, which calls os.Exit(1) and prevents deferred cleanup, closes the process without HTTP 500 responses, and stops the entire container instead of returning an error up the call chain.

Ignoring structured logging: Writing fmt.Printf("User %s logged in", user) instead of slog.Info("user login", "user_id", id, "ip", remoteAddr). Unstructured logs cannot be parsed by log aggregators (Loki, Elastic, Datadog) for search, dashboards, or alert rules.

Logging sensitive data: Learners log request bodies, auth tokens, or SQL queries verbatim. Production pipelines ship logs off-cluster; secrets in logs become compliance violations (PCI, HIPAA, SOC2) and credential leaks.
execution_timeline: 📝 Developer writes structured log call with slog.Info() or logrus.WithField()
⚙️ Go runtime formats the log entry as JSON with timestamp, level, message, and key-value pairs
📤 Log line is written to os.Stdout (or os.Stderr for errors) via the io.Writer interface
🐳 Docker container runtime captures stdout/stderr stream from the process file descriptors
📡 Container runtime forwards the stream to the configured logging driver (json-file, journald, fluentd, awslogs, gelf)
🗄️ Log aggregator (Loki, Elasticsearch, Datadog) ingests the stream, indexes structured fields
🔍 Operator queries logs via LogQL, KQL, or dashboard with filters on level, service, trace_id

failure_modes.scenario: Missing log rotation in file-based logging
failure_modes.cause: App writes to /var/log/app.log without log rotation configured in the app or via logrotate
failure_modes.fix: Always log to stdout in containerized environments. If file logging is unavoidable, use lumberjack or configure logrotate with copytruncate.

failure_modes.scenario: Synchronous log writes block the request goroutine
failure_modes.cause: log.Println() writes synchronously to disk or network; under high throughput this blocks the calling goroutine and increases p99 latency
failure_modes.fix: Use async log writers (logrus.TextFormatter with a buffered writer) or slog's HandlerOptions with a custom async writer. Set log sampling in high-volume paths.

failure_modes.scenario: Panic in deferred log write silences the crash reason
failure_modes.cause: A deferred recover() calls log.Printf but the logger's io.Writer is closed or broken; the Write fails silently and the panic reason is lost
failure_modes.fix: Use slog with a fallback writer. In critical paths, flush logs to stderr with a direct os.Stderr.Write() before os.Exit().

failure_modes.scenario: Log aggregation drops entries due to rate limiting
failure_modes.cause: Aggregator (e.g., Loki) is configured with a per-stream rate limit; a high-traffic service exceeds it and logs are silently dropped
failure_modes.fix: Implement client-side log sampling with slog.HandlerOptions.Level, rate-limit per second with a token bucket, or use a local buffer with backpressure.

failure_modes.scenario: Structured log key collisions in distributed tracing
failure_modes.cause: Multiple services log trace_id under different keys (traceId, trace_id, TraceID); the aggregator treats them as separate fields and cross-service correlation breaks
failure_modes.fix: Adopt a unified logging schema (e.g., OTEL semantic conventions). Use a shared slog.Handler wrapper that enforces canonical key naming.

hidden_magic_checks: When you call log.Fatal() in an HTTP handler, what happens to the response written by a deferred func? (Answer: os.Exit(1) immediately terminates the process; deferred funcs do NOT run after os.Exit.)

Why does Docker capture stdout/stderr but not files written by the application? (Answer: Docker attaches to the container process's stdout/stderr file descriptors. File writes bypass these descriptors unless the app is configured to log to /dev/stdout or /proc/self/fd/1.)

What's the difference between log.Println and slog.Info for a log aggregator consuming the output? (Answer: log.Println emits unstructured text; the aggregator cannot parse fields. slog.Info emits structured JSON; the aggregator indexes key-value pairs for filtering and dashboards.)

If you call log.SetOutput(os.Stderr) but your Dockerfile only captures stdout via CMD ["go", "run", "."], where do the logs go? (Answer: They go to stderr (fd 2). If the Docker CMD or docker logs command only follows fd 1, stderr output is discarded. Always capture both streams or set the logger to os.Stdout.)
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's standard library provides log/slog (structured logging, Go 1.21+) as the idiomatic logger. slog uses a Handler interface that decouples formatting (JSONHandler, TextHandler) from output (io.Writer). The legacy log package writes unstructured text to os.Stderr by default. In production, teams layer on logrus, zerolog, or zap for leveled logging, field chaining, and sampling — but slog's Handler API allows wrapping these as slog backends without changing call sites.
zero_magic.mental_model: Logs are the black box flight recorder for your service. Just as pilots review flight data after an incident, operators query logs to reconstruct request flows, measure latency, and correlate events across services. Each log line is a timestamped sensor reading: structured fields (altitude, speed, heading) let you filter for anomalies; unstructured chatter ("flaps deployed") is noise. The aggregation pipeline is the investigation board — if your sensors don't speak a common format (JSON), the board is useless.
performance_implications: Allocation overhead: slog.Info with 5 key-value pairs allocates ~300 bytes per call. In a 10k req/s service, that is ~3 MB/s in allocations. Use slog.LogAttrs with slog.Attr values to reduce allocs in hot paths.

Blocking writes: log.Println calls os.Stderr.Write synchronously. Under 10k lines/s, the syscall overhead adds ~2ms per write, blocking the caller. Use slog with a buffered or async Handler to batch writes.

JSON serialization: Encoding a structured log entry costs ~500ns on average. At high throughput (100k lines/s), this adds 50ms/s of CPU. Pre-allocate log buffers or use zerolog's zero-allocation JSON encoder.

Sampling overhead: Sampling every 10th log line saves I/O but adds a rand() call per event. Use deterministic hash-based sampling (sample on trace_id) to avoid the rand() syscall and keep correlated traces intact.

Docker log driver throughput: Docker's json-file driver writes each log line as a JSON object to a file. At high volume, this causes disk I/O contention. Switch to the journald driver (binary, compressed) or fluentd (network) to offload disk pressure.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go developers coming from scripting backgrounds (Python, Node.js) use print-debugging (fmt.Println everywhere) and have no mental model for production observability. This lesson bridges that gap by teaching structured logging with log/slog, the 12-factor stdout convention, log aggregation pipelines, and production patterns (levels, sampling, panic/fatal hygiene). The learner stops writing ad-hoc print statements and starts shipping queryable, operational logs.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Given a minimal HTTP server, the learner must: (1) replace all fmt.Println calls with slog structured logging at appropriate levels (DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR), (2) configure the logger to output JSON to stdout with a custom timestamp format, (3) add a middleware that logs request method, path, duration, and status code, (4) demonstrate that log.Fatal is NOT used in the handler (return errors instead), and (5) verify the JSON output can be piped to jq for field extraction.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production Go services at Uber, Datadog, and Grafana Labs use structured logging with slog or zerolog. Kubernetes-native Go apps write JSON logs to stdout; these are collected by fluentd or vector, shipped to Loki or Elasticsearch, and queried via LogQL or Kibana. Common patterns: request-scoped loggers with context.Context (slog.With), trace_id propagation for distributed tracing correlation, sampling in high-volume endpoints (health checks, metrics), and panic handlers that log the stack trace before os.Exit(1).
step_by_step_execution: 1. Create a new directory 14-docker-cicd-linux-deployment/03-logs/
2. Initialize the module: go mod init github.com/the-go-engineer/14-docker-cicd-linux-deployment/03-logs
3. Write main.go: create a slog logger with slog.New(slog.NewJSONHandler(os.Stdout, nil))
4. Add log calls at multiple levels: slog.Debug("starting up"), slog.Info("server listening", "port", 8080), slog.Warn("high memory usage", "pct", memPct), slog.Error("request failed", "err", err)
5. Write an HTTP handler that logs request details with slog.Group and slog.Attr
6. Write main_test.go: start the server, send test requests, capture stdout, and assert JSON log lines contain expected fields
7. Run: go run . and verify JSON output with curl and jq
8. Run tests: go test -v ./...
zero_magic.under_the_hood: slog.NewJSONHandler(os.Stdout, &slog.HandlerOptions{Level: slog.LevelInfo}) returns a handler that encodes each log entry as a JSON object. The handler's Handle() method is called for every enabled log event; it calls encoder.Encode() which writes to the io.Writer. Under the hood, sync.Pool reuses encoder buffers to reduce allocations. The Level type is an int; HandlerOptions.Level is a Leveler interface that can be a dynamic atomic.Int64 for live log level changes. When slog.Info() is called, the logger acquires the handler's mutex, calls Handle(), and returns — this is why synchronous I/O blocks the caller. For async behavior, wrap the handler in a custom writer that sends entries to a channel consumed by a background goroutine.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Logs exist because production software runs without a debugger attached. Print-debugging works in development but fails in distributed, ephemeral, containerized environments where you cannot ssh into a pod and attach delve. Structured logs are the agreed-upon interface between running services and operational tooling (aggregators, alert managers, dashboards). Go's log/slog exists because the legacy log package (from 2009) assumed human-readability; modern infrastructure demands machine-parseable, schema-adherent output that tools can consume without fragile regex parsing.

items.title: File permissions
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply File permissions in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using chmod 0777 as a default to make things work, which bypasses all permission controls and creates security vulnerabilities in production.
Confusing rwx (read/write/execute) octal notation — writing chmod 644 instead of chmod 0644 for a script file, forgetting the execute bit, or using 0777 for directories thinking it mirrors files.
Running containers as root without setting USER in the Dockerfile, causing all created files and logs to be owned by root and inaccessible to non-root CI/CD or host processes.
Assuming os.WriteFile defaults to a secure permission — it uses 0666 (before umask) by default unless you explicitly pass os.FileMode(0644), which means secrets or config files can be world-readable.
Forgetting that umask subtracts from the mode passed to os.OpenFile or os.WriteFile, then wondering why the resulting file has different permissions than the octal value they passed.
zero_magic.execution_timeline: 1️⃣ Check current file permissions with os.Stat → 2️⃣ Select appropriate octal mode (0644 for files, 0755 for directories/scripts) → 3️⃣ Open or create file with os.OpenFile specifying the FileMode → 4️⃣ After write, verify with os.Lstat → 5️⃣ If running in Docker, add USER appuser and chown artifacts → 6️⃣ Use umask to strip group/world bits from newly created files → 7️⃣ Validate no world-writable files exist in production image

failure_modes.cause: Used os.WriteFile with 0777 or umask was set too permissive
failure_modes.fix: Use os.FileMode(0600) for secrets and verify umask is 0022 or stricter; audit with a CI check that rejects files with group/world write

failure_modes.failure: Container runs as root, log files owned by uid 0
failure_modes.cause: No USER directive in Dockerfile; default is root
failure_modes.fix: Add USER 1000 in Dockerfile; chown -R the working directory to uid 1000 before switching; use os.Chown on created files

failure_modes.failure: Executable script copied to container loses execute bit
failure_modes.cause: COPY preserves permissions from build context; git may not track execute bits on Windows
failure_modes.fix: Explicitly os.Chmod the file to 0755 in the Dockerfile RUN layer or in Go init(); ensure .gitattributes defines * text=auto eol=lf

failure_modes.failure: Setuid/setgid bit left on an executable that should not have it
failure_modes.cause: Accidentally preserved setuid from a tarball extraction — for example, chmod 4755 was used
failure_modes.fix: Use os.FileMode(0755) without the setuid flag; run chmod u-s in Dockerfile or os.Chmod(path, 0755) in Go; add a security scanner step

hidden_magic_checks.question: If umask is 0027 and you call os.OpenFile("data", os.O_RDWR|os.O_CREATE, 0640), what are the resulting file permissions?
hidden_magic_checks.answer: The umask subtracts group-w and all others: 0640 &^ 0027 = 0640 becomes 0640 (rw-r-----). Group-w is already absent in 0640 so umask has no visible effect here.

hidden_magic_checks.question: Why does os.WriteFile("key.pem", data, 0644) expose the private key to every user on the system?
hidden_magic_checks.answer: 0644 is world-readable. Private keys must use 0600 (owner-read-write only). Never use 0644 or above for secrets.

hidden_magic_checks.question: What happens when a Docker container is started with --read-only root filesystem but the application writes logs to /var/log/app?
hidden_magic_checks.answer: The write fails with read-only file system error because the root filesystem is immutable. Fix: mount a tmpfs or volume at /var/log/app, or write to a directory mounted as writable.

hidden_magic_checks.question: When does chmod 4777 on a Go binary inside /usr/local/bin cause a security vulnerability?
hidden_magic_checks.answer: The setuid bit (4xxx) makes the binary run with the owner's privileges. If root owns it, any user executing it gains root-level access — a classic privilege-escalation vector. Never set setuid on custom binaries unless you have a verified, security-reviewed reason.

zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's os package models file permissions with the os.FileMode type (a uint32 wrapping Unix permission bits). os.Stat returns FileMode; os.OpenFile accepts it as the perm argument; os.Chmod sets it. The fs.FileMode interface allows testing with .IsDir(), .Perm(), .IsRegular(). Go's os.WriteFile defaults to 0666 (before umask), which is rarely what you want — always pass an explicit FileMode. The os.FileMode bit layout mirrors the Unix model: lower 9 bits for owner/group/other rwx, bits 11-12 for setuid/setgid, bit 10 for sticky. Go's os.Stat, os.Lstat, os.Chmod, os.Chown, and os.Chtimes make permission inspection and manipulation idiomatic from Go without shelling out to chmod or chown. The standard library's filepath.Walk and fs.WalkDir can filter by permission bits to find world-writable files or verify ownership. In tests, you set up temp directories with t.TempDir() (which creates with 0700) and use os.Chmod to simulate restricted environments.
zero_magic.mental_model: Think of permissions as three concentric rings of trust: Owner (inner ring, full control), Group (middle ring, shared access), and Others (outer ring, public). Each ring has a three-bit mask (rwx). Octal notation encodes all three rings in one number: owner-group-other. The sticky bit on /tmp means only the owner of a file can delete it. Setuid means run this with the file owner's privileges regardless of who executes it. Go's FileMode is the language's direct port of this Unix model — when you see os.FileMode(0644), read it as owner writes and reads, everyone else only reads.
performance_implications: Each os.Stat or os.Lstat call involves a syscall to the kernel; batching permission checks (for example, walk once with fs.WalkDir instead of repeated Stat calls) reduces syscall overhead in hot paths.
os.Chmod on a frequently-written log file causes a metadata write to the filesystem journal; if done per-log-line, it kills throughput — set permissions once on creation.
Docker images with many os.Chown or os.Chmod layers increase image size because each RUN chown creates a new layer; combine ownership changes into a single layer.
Overly restrictive permissions (for example, 0400) on a database file cause open/read errors at runtime that are visible only under load when files are first accessed.
Using os.TempDir (creates with 0700) is safer but slower if used per-operation because it makes a stat + mkdir syscall each time; reuse a single temp directory with unique file names instead.
zero_magic.problem_solved: File permissions solve the problem of access control in multi-tenant operating systems and container environments: they prevent one user or process from reading, modifying, or executing files owned by another. Without permissions, any process could read SSH private keys, overwrite binaries, or delete critical system files. In Go, explicit permission handling prevents production incidents where misconfigured file modes lead to data leaks, privilege escalation, or silent deployment failures.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Write a Go program that creates a directory ~/secure-workspace with 0700 permissions, writes a config.json file inside it with 0600, then attempts to read it from a child process that runs as a different (non-existent) UID and handles the resulting permission-denied error gracefully. Explain why tmpdirs in Go default to 0700 and how umask interacts with os.OpenFile.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Go production services use os.FileMode extensively: HTTP servers restrict TLS key files to 0600, CI/CD pipelines enforce permission audits in pull requests, Kubernetes init containers chown volumes to non-root UIDs, logging libraries open log files with 0644 (or 0600 for audit logs), and os.Stat checks gate access to config directories in multi-tenant API servers. The Go linter gosec (G301/G302/G306) flags missing or overly-permissive FileMode arguments in os.WriteFile, os.OpenFile, and os.Chmod calls — preventing 0777 or 0644 on secrets before they reach production.
step_by_step_execution: Step 1: Run go run main.go with a file that creates a secret file using os.WriteFile and explicitly pass os.FileMode(0600) for the key file and os.FileMode(0644) for a non-sensitive config file.
Step 2: Inspect the created file permissions with ls -la on Linux or Get-ChildItem on Windows (Go abstracts underlying permissions but the interface stays the same).
Step 3: Use os.Stat inside the program and call .Mode().Perm() to retrieve the numeric permission bits; print them as octal with fmt.Printf("%o", mode.Perm()).

Step 4: Run the same program inside a Docker container (FROM golang:1.22-alpine) built with USER 1000 and observe that os.WriteFile still works correctly because permissions are relative to the UID, not the container boundary.

Step 5: Add os.Chmod to reduce permissions of an already-written config file from 0644 to 0600 after a config value validation; verify the change with os.Lstat (note: Lstat does not follow symlinks — important for /proc or /etc hosts).

Step 6: Apply umask by calling syscall.Umask(0027) at startup and verify that os.OpenFile with 0640 produces a file with permissions 0640 (because group-w is already absent) but os.OpenFile with 0666 produces 0640 (group-w is stripped).

Step 7: Test setuid behavior by creating a small Go binary that prints os.Getuid(), building it with os.FileMode(0755), setting setuid via os.Chmod(path, os.FileMode(04555)), and observing that the kernel ignores setuid on Go binaries with the nosuid mount option or in Docker by default.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go's os.FileMode is a uint32 where the lower 12 bits mirror the Unix permission bitmap: bits 0-2 are other rwx, bits 3-5 are group rwx, bits 6-8 are owner rwx, bit 9 is sticky (S_ISVTX), bit 10 is setgid (S_ISGID), bit 11 is setuid (S_ISUID). The os.Stat syscall on Linux fills a syscall.Stat_t struct whose Mode field is uint32 — Go casts it to os.FileMode. On Windows, os.FileMode still exists but the permission bits (owner/group/other) are largely ignored; Go maps the Windows file attributes to a simplified mode where regular files get 0666 and directories get 0777. The os.Chmod on Windows translates to SetFileAttributes. Docker layers each add their own metadata overlay: when you RUN chmod in a Dockerfile, the overlay filesystem's metadata layer records the new mode without rewriting the underlying file data. When os.Chmod is called inside a running container, it operates on the overlay's copy-on-write layer. Understanding that umask is a kernel-level process attribute (set per-process with syscall.Umask) explains why two Go programs in the same container can see different effective permissions — umask is inherited across fork/exec.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: File permissions exist because Unix was designed as a multi-user system from the ground up (1970s Bell Labs). They provide the simplest enforceable access control model that scales from a single developer laptop to a 10,000-node Kubernetes cluster. Without permissions, any process — or any compromised dependency — could read, modify, or delete any file on the system. In the Go ecosystem, explicit FileMode handling is the last line of defense against accidental data exposure: a missing or overly-permissive FileMode in os.WriteFile is how private keys, database passwords, and TLS certificates end up world-readable in production.

items.title: Networking basics
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Networking basics in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using Networking basics without understanding the underlying mechanism leads to fragile code that breaks on edge cases.
Applying Networking basics in contexts where it doesn't belong, creating unnecessary complexity.
Copying Networking basics patterns from examples without understanding why they work or when they fail.
execution_timeline: The compiler validates the Networking basics syntax and type constraints against the Go specification.
At runtime, the Networking basics mechanism initializes and follows Go's evaluation order and memory model.
Edge cases and error conditions are handled according to Go's explicit error-handling conventions.%!(EXTRA string=Networking basics)

failure_modes.scenario: A developer uses Networking basics without considering nil or zero-value inputs.
failure_modes.cause: Go initializes variables to zero values which may not be valid for all Networking basics operations.
failure_modes.fix: Always validate inputs and handle zero/nil cases before operating on Networking basics values.

failure_modes.scenario: A team adopts a Networking basics pattern that works in isolation but fails in concurrent scenarios.
failure_modes.cause: The Networking basics implementation was not designed with goroutine safety in mind.
failure_modes.fix: Add proper synchronization or document that Networking basics is not safe for concurrent use.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict the behavior when Networking basics encounters an unexpected or invalid input.
Learner must explain why two seemingly identical Networking basics implementations produce different results.
Learner must identify a subtle bug in a Networking basics example and fix it without changing the public API.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's standard library and ecosystem use Networking basics extensively. The language's design philosophy favors explicit, composable abstractions, and Networking basics reflects this approach in production code, tests, and tooling.
zero_magic.mental_model: Think of Networking basics as a well-defined contract between parts of your program. When used correctly, it reduces cognitive load by encapsulating complexity behind a clear interface. The mental model should focus on inputs, outputs, and invariant guarantees rather than implementation details.
performance_implications: The performance cost of Networking basics is typically negligible compared to I/O and other real-world bottlenecks.
Avoid premature optimization with Networking basics — write clear idiomatic code first, then profile and optimize if needed.
Some Networking basics patterns can cause unexpected allocations or blocking — benchmark hot paths to verify performance.
zero_magic.problem_solved: This lesson explains what problem Networking basics solves for Go developers — specifically, how it enables clearer, safer, and more maintainable code compared to alternative approaches.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must complete a focused exercise that applies Networking basics in a realistic scenario, then explain their implementation choices and the tradeoffs involved.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Professional Go developers use Networking basics in production services, CLI tools, API servers, and data pipelines. Understanding Networking basics is essential for writing idiomatic, maintainable Go code that teams can confidently deploy and extend.
step_by_step_execution: The Go compiler parses the Networking basics syntax and validates type constraints.
The runtime initializes the necessary data structures following Go's memory model.%!(EXTRA string=Networking basics)
The operation executes according to Go's evaluation order and control flow semantics.%!(EXTRA string=Networking basics)
Edge cases and error conditions trigger Go's built-in error handling mechanisms (error values, panic/recover, or both).%!(EXTRA string=Networking basics)
The result is returned or made available for the next step, with proper cleanup via defer if needed.%!(EXTRA string=Networking basics)
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Under the hood, Go's Networking basics follows the language specification's semantics. The Go runtime and compiler work together to ensure correct behavior, memory safety, and predictable performance. Understanding the runtime behavior helps developers reason about edge cases and optimize critical code paths.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Networking basics exists because real Go software needs this concept to manage complexity, ensure correctness, and enable team collaboration. Go's design philosophy favors clarity and simplicity, and Networking basics reflects that approach by providing a focused, well-defined solution to a specific class of programming problems.
