items.title: Docker basics
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Docker basics in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using `ubuntu:latest` as the base image instead of `golang:alpine` or `scratch`, producing a 800 MB+ image for a 15 MB Go binary.
Running `go build` inside the container without `CGO_ENABLED=0` and `-ldflags="-s -w"`, resulting in a binary linked against glibc that crashes on `scratch` or `alpine`.
Copying the entire source tree with `COPY . .` instead of using `.dockerignore` or a multi-stage `COPY --from=build`, bloating layers with cached Go modules and `.git` history.
Using `CMD ./myapp` instead of the exec form `CMD ["./myapp"]`, causing signals (SIGTERM) to be swallowed by the shell wrapper and preventing graceful shutdown.
Forgetting `--chmod` on COPY or running as root, leaving the container with a security profile that violates the principle of least privilege.
execution_timeline: ✏️ Developer writes a Dockerfile with `FROM golang:1.22-alpine AS build` and `FROM scratch`.
⚙️ Docker daemon reads the Dockerfile and creates a build context from the directory (less anything in `.dockerignore`).
📦 The builder executes each instruction: `WORKDIR /app`, `COPY go.mod go.sum .`, `RUN go mod download` (layers cached).
🔨 `COPY . .` then `RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 go build -ldflags="-s -w" -o /app/server .` produces a static binary.
🗃️ `FROM scratch` starts a fresh empty layer — nothing carried from the build stage except the copied binary.
📎 `COPY --from=build /app/server /server` — the only file in the final image.
🏷️ `docker build -t myapp:latest .` tags the image.
🚀 `docker run -d -p 8080:8080 --name myapp myapp:latest` starts the container.
🔍 `docker exec myapp ./healthcheck` runs an ad-hoc command in the running container.
🧹 `docker stop myapp` sends SIGTERM directly because CMD used exec form.

failure_modes.cause: Using `FROM golang:1.22` (full Debian) without multi-stage — final image is 900 MB.
failure_modes.fix: Switch to multi-stage: `FROM golang:1.22-alpine AS build` + `FROM scratch`.
failure_modes.scenario: Deploying a 900 MB image to AWS ECS causes slow pull times and higher cost.

failure_modes.cause: Building with CGO enabled when targeting scratch; binary segfaults because `libc.so.6` is missing.
failure_modes.fix: Set `CGO_ENABLED=0` and use `netgo` tags if needed: `-tags netgo -installsuffix netgo`.
failure_modes.scenario: CI pipeline builds a Go binary, pushes to Docker Hub, but the container exits immediately with 'standard_init_linux.go: exec user process caused: no such file or directory'.

failure_modes.cause: Using `CMD server` (shell form) prevents SIGTERM propagation; container stops with `docker kill` instead of `docker stop`.
failure_modes.fix: Use exec form: `CMD ["./server"]` or `ENTRYPOINT ["./server"]`.
failure_modes.scenario: Orchestrator (K8s) sends SIGTERM, container ignores it, K8s escalates to SIGKILL after 30 s, causing dirty shutdowns.

failure_modes.cause: No `.dockerignore` file; `.git`, `node_modules`, `vendor/` get sent to the Docker daemon, inflating the build context to 100 MB+.
failure_modes.fix: Add `.dockerignore` with `.git`, `*.md`, `vendor/`, `tmp/`, `coverage.out`.
failure_modes.scenario: Slow `docker build` on CI takes 3 minutes because the daemon must parse and hash 50k unnecessary files.

failure_modes.cause: COPY instead of ADD for remote URLs or tar auto-extraction when not intended; COPY is preferred for local files.
failure_modes.fix: Use `COPY` for local files, `ADD` only when you need automatic tar extraction or URL download.
failure_modes.scenario: `ADD --chmod=0755 https://example.com/plugin.tar.gz /plugins/` unexpectedly fetches at build time, making the build non-reproducible.

hidden_magic_checks.answer: No. `docker exec` starts a new process inside the container, but only the ENTRYPOINT/CMD process is the PID 1 that receives signals.
hidden_magic_checks.question: If I run `docker run --rm myapp` and the Go binary listens on :8080, can I use `docker exec -it myapp sh` to modify the binary while the server is running?

hidden_magic_checks.answer: Because Go can produce a statically-linked binary with `CGO_ENABLED=0` that runs on `scratch` — a completely empty image with zero vulnerabilities.
hidden_magic_checks.question: Why is `FROM scratch` a practical option for Go but not for Python or Node.js applications?

hidden_magic_checks.answer: Docker caches layers. If `COPY go.mod go.sum .` doesn't change, the `RUN go mod download` layer is reused from cache, saving minutes per rebuild.
hidden_magic_checks.question: Why should I COPY `go.mod` and `go.sum` before the rest of the source code in a Dockerfile?

hidden_magic_checks.answer: No — `alpine` uses musl libc. Build with `CGO_ENABLED=0` to produce a fully static binary, or install `gcompat`/build with `CGO_ENABLED=1` and `-tags musl`.
hidden_magic_checks.question: If I compile on my macOS (Darwin) and COPY the binary into a `golang:alpine` image, will it run?

zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's standard toolchain makes it uniquely suited for Docker. With `CGO_ENABLED=0`, `go build` produces a static binary with no external dependencies. The Go community standard practice is multi-stage builds: build with `golang:alpine`, deploy on `scratch` or `distroless`. Projects like Docker itself, Kubernetes, Prometheus, and Caddy all use this pattern. Go's vendoring and module cache also interact well with Docker layer caching — `go mod download` can be cached separately from the source COPY.
zero_magic.mental_model: Think of Docker as a filesystem-augmented process launcher. A container is just a Linux process isolated by namespaces (PID, network, mount, UTS, IPC) and constrained by cgroups. The Dockerfile is a recipe that assembles a read-only filesystem snapshot (image) — each instruction adds a layer (an overlayfs diff). When you run a container, Docker overlays a writable layer on top and executes ENTRYPOINT/CMD as PID 1. For Go developers, the mental model simplifies: the binary + its layer = the running process. Everything else is packaging detail.
performance_implications: Image size: A `scratch`-based Go image can be as small as 5–15 MB vs 800+ MB for a full Debian base; smaller images pull faster, start faster, and have a smaller attack surface.
Layer caching: Order Dockerfile instructions from least-frequently-changing (go.mod, go.sum) to most-frequently-changing (source code) to maximise cache reuse and reduce build times by 60-80%.
Build speed: `docker build` parallelism (BuildKit) can execute independent stages and COPY operations concurrently; enable BuildKit with `DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1`.
Multi-stage elimination: Every COPY --from=build carries only needed artifacts; unused layers from the build stage are discarded, keeping the final image lean.
Memory limits: Use `docker run --memory=256m --cpus=0.5` to prevent a Go binary with a GC spike from starving other containers on the same host.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Docker solves environment reproducibility. Before Docker, every team member and CI server needed to install Go, MySQL, Redis, and system libraries at matching versions. Docker packages the Go binary, its runtime dependencies (or lack thereof with `scratch`), and the execution environment into a single immutable artifact. This eliminates 'works on my machine' and makes deployments atomic — you ship an image, not a tarball of binaries with install instructions.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Write a Dockerfile that takes a Go HTTP server (which listens on :8080 and serves `{"status":"ok"}` at `/healthz`), builds it with CGO disabled, and produces a final image under 20 MB. Then run it with `docker run -p 8080:8080 --rm my-go-server` and verify the health endpoint responds. Then explain in one paragraph why the image is so small.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every major Go project uses Docker for delivery. Docker Hub lists official images for Go itself, as well as Go-based tools like Traefik, Caddy, Hugo, and Syncthing. In production, Go services are deployed as Docker containers on ECS, GKE, AKS, or EKS — often built via CI (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI) and pushed to a registry, then pulled by the orchestrator. `docker-compose.yml` is used for local development to spin up the Go API alongside Postgres, Redis, and a message broker.
step_by_step_execution: Create a `.dockerignore` listing `.git`, `vendor/`, `*.md`, `tmp/`, `coverage.out`, `Dockerfile`.
Write a `Dockerfile` with `FROM golang:1.22-alpine AS build` and set `WORKDIR /app`.
Copy dependency files first: `COPY go.mod go.sum ./` then `RUN go mod download`.
Copy source: `COPY . .` and build: `RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 go build -ldflags="-s -w" -o /server .`.
Start the minimal stage: `FROM scratch`. Copy the binary: `COPY --from=build /server /server`.
Set the runtime command in exec form: `CMD ["/server"]`.
Build: `docker build -t my-go-server .` and check size with `docker images my-go-server`.
Run: `docker run -d -p 8080:8080 --name my-go-server my-go-server`.
Test: `curl http://localhost:8080/healthz`.
Debug with `docker exec -it my-go-server sh` (or use `docker run --rm -it --entrypoint sh my-go-server` for scratch images where no shell exists).
Stop with `docker stop my-go-server && docker rm my-go-server`.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Docker is a client-server architecture. The `docker` CLI talks to `dockerd` (the daemon) via a REST API (by default on `/var/run/docker.sock`). When you run `docker build`, the daemon receives the build context (tarred), unpack it, and parses the Dockerfile instruction-by-instruction. Each instruction becomes a layer: a commit of the filesystem diff using overlayfs (or aufs, devicemapper, etc.). Layers are content-addressed and cached. When you `docker run` an image, the daemon creates a new mount namespace, overlays the image layers + a writable layer, sets up networking (bridge by default), then forks the ENTRYPOINT/CMD as PID 1 in that namespace. The Go binary runs as PID 1 — it handles all signals directly (a key reason Go and Docker work well together, since Go's runtime handles SIGTERM, SIGINT cleanly).
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Docker basics exist because modern software deployment requires immutable, reproducible, and portable application packaging. A Go binary is inherently static, making Docker an ideal delivery mechanism — but only when the developer understands base images, multi-stage builds, signal handling with exec vs shell form, and image size hygiene. Without this knowledge, a Go developer will produce slow-build, oversized, insecure, or non-restartable containers that defeat the purpose of containerization.

items.title: Docker images and layers
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Docker images and layers in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Putting RUN go build and COPY source in the same layer, defeating layer caching and forcing a full rebuild on every source change.
Using alpine as the final base image when a scratch image would suffice, ignoring that Go static binaries need nothing from the OS.
Not ordering Dockerfile instructions from least-frequently-changing to most-frequently-changing, causing unnecessary cache invalidation on every build.
Copying the entire build context (including .git, node_modules, local binaries) into the image, inflating both build time and final image size.
Believing distroless images are always smaller than alpine without checking — distroless includes libc and security tooling, while scratch is truly empty.
zero_magic.execution_timeline:
→ Developer writes multi-stage Dockerfile
→ docker build reads Dockerfile and sends build context to daemon (excluding .dockerignore)
→ Docker resolves base image: FROM golang:1.22 AS builder
→ Each instruction executes as a new read-only layer
→ Cached layers are reused when instruction + parent ID + file checksums match
→ RUN go mod download is cached independently from source code
→ COPY . . invalidates all subsequent layers if any source file changed
→ RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 go build produces a statically linked binary (~10MB)
→ Second stage starts FROM scratch
→ COPY --from=builder copies only the binary
→ Final image is ~10MB with exactly 2 layers
→ docker history myapp shows each layer, its creation instruction, and its size

failure_modes.scenario: Docker build cache miss on every commit because go.mod is copied after all source files
failure_modes.cause: COPY . . comes before COPY go.mod go.sum and RUN go mod download, so any source change invalidates the dependency layer
failure_modes.fix: Reorder: COPY go.mod go.sum . first, RUN go mod download, then COPY . . to preserve the dependency cache

failure_modes.scenario: Final image is 800MB+ containing the Go toolchain and source code
failure_modes.cause: Single-stage build using golang:1.22 as the final base image instead of multi-stage with scratch
failure_modes.fix: Use multi-stage build: FROM golang:1.22 AS builder, then FROM scratch, COPY --from=builder /app/main /main

failure_modes.scenario: Go binary built in Docker fails with 'not found' or 'exec format error' on scratch
failure_modes.cause: Binary was compiled with CGO_ENABLED=1 producing a dynamic binary that needs libc, or for the wrong architecture
failure_modes.fix: Build with CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux GOARCH=amd64 go build -o /app/main . and use scratch or gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12 if cgo is required

failure_modes.scenario: Secrets like .env or SSH keys leak in the final image despite being deleted mid-build
failure_modes.cause: Docker layers are additive; deleting a file in a later layer leaves the original layer (with the secret) in the image history
failure_modes.fix: Use Docker BuildKit secrets: RUN --mount=type=secret,id=mysecret … or multi-stage build that never copies secrets into intermediate layers

failure_modes.scenario: docker build fails with 'no space left on device'
failure_modes.cause: Accumulated cached intermediate layers and dangling images consume all available disk space
failure_modes.fix: Run docker system prune -f and docker buildx prune -f regularly; use --no-cache for release builds to avoid unbounded cache growth

hidden_magic_checks: Check 1: Q: Why does removing a file in a later Docker layer not reduce the final image size? A: Docker layers are additive and immutable. The layer that originally added the file persists in the image history. The delete layer only adds a whiteout entry — the underlying data is still present in the layer below.

Check 2: Q: What is the caching difference between COPY and ADD for Go builds? A: COPY only copies local files and its cache key is the checksum of source files. ADD supports URL downloads and tar auto-extraction, which can produce unpredictable cache keys from network content. COPY is preferred for reproducible Go builds.

Check 3: Q: Your scratch image's Go binary crashes with 'exec format error'. What is the most likely cause? A: The binary was compiled for the wrong architecture/OS (e.g., darwin/arm64 instead of linux/amd64) or was dynamically linked. Verify GOOS, GOARCH, and CGO_ENABLED=0.

Check 4: Q: How does docker build determine whether to reuse a cached layer? A: It compares the instruction text, the checksum of files referenced by COPY/ADD, and the parent layer ID. If all three match identically, the cached layer is reused and the builder proceeds to the next instruction.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go developers use multi-stage Docker builds as the de facto standard for shipping production services. The canonical Go Dockerfile separates dependency resolution (go mod download) from source compilation, producing a statically linked binary that runs on scratch — the absolute smallest possible base image. This pattern is universal across the Go ecosystem: Docker CLI, Kubernetes, Prometheus, Grafana, Caddy, Traefik, and every HashiCorp tool rely on it.
zero_magic.mental_model: Think of Docker images as a stack of transparent overlays, each recording only the filesystem changes from the one below. When a process reads a file, Docker's union filesystem (overlayfs) walks the stack top-to-bottom and returns the first version it finds. Deleting a file in a later overlay does not erase it from earlier overlays — it just places a 'hidden' marker on top. This mental model explains why a 800MB build stage can yield an 8MB final image, why deleting intermediate files mid-build does nothing to shrink the image, and why reordering instructions changes cache behavior.
performance_implications: Layer caching: Well-ordered layers (dependencies before source) reduce iterative build time by 80–95% because unchanged dependency layers skip re-execution entirely.

Image size: Multi-stage builds with scratch shrink production images from ~800MB (full Go toolchain) to ~10MB (single static binary), lowering network transfer, registry storage, and container cold-start time.

Build context size: Every file in the build context is sent to the Docker daemon and checksummed for COPY cache invalidation. Adding a .dockerignore to exclude .git, node_modules, and local binaries can cut context size by 90%+.

Cache invalidation: Changing go.mod invalidates the go mod download layer and all subsequent layers. Grouping dependency copying and downloading into dedicated early layers minimizes unnecessary rebuilds during source iteration.
Layer count and metadata: Each layer adds metadata and extraction overhead. While overlay2 has no hard layer limit, fewer layers mean faster docker pull extraction and lower image manifest size.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Docker images and layers solve the problem of shipping Go applications with reproducible, minimal, and cache-efficient builds. Before layers, every Docker build was monolithic — any source change triggered a full re-download of dependencies and recompilation from scratch. Before multi-stage, production images carried the full Go toolchain. Layers give developers fine-grained cache granularity and the ability to compose images from reusable filesystem diffs, making iterative development fast and production images as small as possible.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Create a Dockerfile that builds a Go HTTP server in under 10MB using multi-stage build with properly ordered layers and a .dockerignore. Run docker build -t go-server ., then docker history go-server to list each layer with its size. Write a single paragraph explaining why each COPY, RUN, and FROM instruction is ordered the way it is and how the layer cache behaves when go.mod changes vs. when only a .go source file changes.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every major Go project that ships as a Docker image uses multi-stage builds with scratch or distroless. Docker CLI, Kubernetes (kubelet, kube-apiserver, etcd), Prometheus, Grafana, Caddy, Traefik, and HashiCorp tools (Consul, Vault, Nomad, Terraform) all follow this pattern. The Go Dockerfile is so universally clean that Docker's official documentation uses it as the canonical multi-stage example.
step_by_step_execution: 1. Create a .dockerignore excluding .git, node_modules, *.local, and any build artifacts.
2. Start the builder stage: FROM golang:1.22-alpine AS builder.
3. Set WORKDIR /build and copy dependency files first: COPY go.mod go.sum ./.
4. Download dependencies: RUN go mod download (this layer is cached unless go.mod or go.sum change).
5. Copy all remaining source code: COPY . . (this layer invalidates on any source change).
6. Build a static binary: RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux GOARCH=amd64 go build -o /app/main .
7. Start the runtime stage: FROM scratch.
8. Copy only the binary: COPY --from=builder /app/main /main.
9. Set the entrypoint: ENTRYPOINT ["/main"].
10. Build: docker build -t myservice . && docker history myservice to inspect layer sizes.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Docker images are stored under /var/lib/docker/overlay2 (Linux) as a set of read-only snapshots, each corresponding to one Dockerfile instruction. Each layer is a tarball of filesystem diffs. The union mount — overlayfs — combines all layers into a merged view via a lowerdir (image layers) and upperdir (container writable layer). When a container writes a file, the copy-on-write mechanism copies the file from a lower layer to the upper layer. docker history decompiles an image into its instruction list with per-layer sizes built from the diff metadata. The --squash flag merges all layers into one, trading cache granularity for a flatter, smaller image at the cost of losing the ability to share intermediate layers across images.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without layers, every Docker build would be monolithic: any source change would force refetching all dependencies, recompiling everything, and rebuilding the entire image from scratch. Layers exist to enable incremental, cacheable, and parallelizable image construction. They are the foundation of Docker's build speed and the multi-stage pattern that makes Go production images the smallest possible — a scratch-based Go image can be as small as 5–10 MB, impossible without the layer abstraction.

items.title: Multi-stage builds
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Multi-stage builds in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using Multi-stage builds without understanding the underlying mechanism leads to fragile code that breaks on edge cases.
Applying Multi-stage builds in contexts where it doesn't belong, creating unnecessary complexity.
Copying Multi-stage builds patterns from examples without understanding why they work or when they fail.
execution_timeline: The compiler validates the Multi-stage builds syntax and type constraints against the Go specification.
At runtime, the Multi-stage builds mechanism initializes and follows Go's evaluation order and memory model.
Edge cases and error conditions are handled according to Go's explicit error-handling conventions.%!(EXTRA string=Multi-stage builds)

failure_modes.scenario: A developer uses Multi-stage builds without considering nil or zero-value inputs.
failure_modes.cause: Go initializes variables to zero values which may not be valid for all Multi-stage builds operations.
failure_modes.fix: Always validate inputs and handle zero/nil cases before operating on Multi-stage builds values.

failure_modes.scenario: A team adopts a Multi-stage builds pattern that works in isolation but fails in concurrent scenarios.
failure_modes.cause: The Multi-stage builds implementation was not designed with goroutine safety in mind.
failure_modes.fix: Add proper synchronization or document that Multi-stage builds is not safe for concurrent use.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict the behavior when Multi-stage builds encounters an unexpected or invalid input.
Learner must explain why two seemingly identical Multi-stage builds implementations produce different results.
Learner must identify a subtle bug in a Multi-stage builds example and fix it without changing the public API.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's standard library and ecosystem use Multi-stage builds extensively. The language's design philosophy favors explicit, composable abstractions, and Multi-stage builds reflects this approach in production code, tests, and tooling.
zero_magic.mental_model: Think of Multi-stage builds as a well-defined contract between parts of your program. When used correctly, it reduces cognitive load by encapsulating complexity behind a clear interface. The mental model should focus on inputs, outputs, and invariant guarantees rather than implementation details.
performance_implications: The performance cost of Multi-stage builds is typically negligible compared to I/O and other real-world bottlenecks.
Avoid premature optimization with Multi-stage builds — write clear idiomatic code first, then profile and optimize if needed.
Some Multi-stage builds patterns can cause unexpected allocations or blocking — benchmark hot paths to verify performance.
zero_magic.problem_solved: This lesson explains what problem Multi-stage builds solves for Go developers — specifically, how it enables clearer, safer, and more maintainable code compared to alternative approaches.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must complete a focused exercise that applies Multi-stage builds in a realistic scenario, then explain their implementation choices and the tradeoffs involved.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Professional Go developers use Multi-stage builds in production services, CLI tools, API servers, and data pipelines. Understanding Multi-stage builds is essential for writing idiomatic, maintainable Go code that teams can confidently deploy and extend.
step_by_step_execution: The Go compiler parses the Multi-stage builds syntax and validates type constraints.
The runtime initializes the necessary data structures following Go's memory model.%!(EXTRA string=Multi-stage builds)
The operation executes according to Go's evaluation order and control flow semantics.%!(EXTRA string=Multi-stage builds)
Edge cases and error conditions trigger Go's built-in error handling mechanisms (error values, panic/recover, or both).%!(EXTRA string=Multi-stage builds)
The result is returned or made available for the next step, with proper cleanup via defer if needed.%!(EXTRA string=Multi-stage builds)
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Under the hood, Go's Multi-stage builds follows the language specification's semantics. The Go runtime and compiler work together to ensure correct behavior, memory safety, and predictable performance. Understanding the runtime behavior helps developers reason about edge cases and optimize critical code paths.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Multi-stage builds exists because real Go software needs this concept to manage complexity, ensure correctness, and enable team collaboration. Go's design philosophy favors clarity and simplicity, and Multi-stage builds reflects that approach by providing a focused, well-defined solution to a specific class of programming problems.

items.title: Opslane tenant isolation
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Opslane tenant isolation in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Adding WHERE tenant_id = ? to queries but forgetting it in one query — a single un-scoped query leaks all tenants' data to every tenant, and the bug is invisible until a customer reports seeing another tenant's data.
Using tenant_id from the URL path instead of the authenticated session — a tenant A user can change the URL path to tenant B's ID and access B's data if the middleware does not validate ownership.
Sharing a single connection pool across all tenants — one tenant's slow query blocks the pool, starving all other tenants and causing cross-tenant latency impact.

Including tenant_id in error messages or logs — a stack trace with tenant-specific identifiers in a shared log aggregator leaks tenant information to the on-call engineer who may not have authorization for that tenant.
Using a single database schema for all tenants without a tenant_id column — adding tenant isolation after launch requires a full data migration with downtime.
execution_timeline: Tenant A admin sends GET /api/orders with session token SESS_A
→ Auth middleware extracts tenant_id=A from session, stores in context
→ Handler reads orders from context: ctx.Value(tenantKey).(string) = 'A'
→ Repository appends WHERE tenant_id = 'A' to every query
→ Database returns only orders belonging to tenant A
→ Response is sent to tenant A — no tenant B data is visible
→ Tenant B admin sends same request with SESS_B
→ Auth middleware extracts tenant_id=B, stores in context
→ Handler uses tenant_id=B, repository scopes all queries to B
→ Both tenants see only their own data, isolated by the tenant_id from the session
failure_modes: Tenant leak via missing WHERE clause: a new endpoint is added without the tenant_id filter — a tenant A user can query /api/admin/all-orders and see every tenant's orders, a P0 security breach.
Tenant leak via error response: a query fails with a PostgreSQL error that includes the tenant_id in the error message — the API returns the error message verbatim, leaking tenant_id to the caller.

Cross-tenant timing attack: tenant isolation is enforced at the application layer but the database connection pool is shared — tenant A's slow query blocks the pool, causing tenant B's fast queries to time out, leaking the fact that tenant A is under load.
Tenant enumeration: a public endpoint returns different error messages for 'tenant exists' vs 'tenant does not exist' — an attacker can enumerate valid tenant IDs.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must write a test that would catch a tenant-leak bug before seeing the production fix.
Learner must explain why a shared connection pool per tenant is harder to diagnose than a dedicated pool per tenant.
Learner must identify the bug in a codebase where one query in a handler is missing the tenant_id filter — without seeing the full handler code, just the repository layer.

Learner must design a tenant middleware that extracts tenant_id from the JWT, validates it against the user's session, and stores it in context — covering the edge case where the JWT tenant_id does not match the session tenant_id.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's context.Context is the idiomatic mechanism for tenant propagation. A middleware extracts the tenant ID from the JWT (or session) and stores it in context using a typed key. All repository methods accept context and extract the tenant ID. ORMs like sqlx and GORM support query hooks that automatically append tenant_id filters. Testing tenant isolation is done by creating users in two tenants and verifying that each user sees only their own data, even when the other tenant's data is accessed via direct IDs.
zero_magic.mental_model: Tenant isolation is a data firewall between tenants. Every database query must include a tenant_id filter. Every cache key must include the tenant_id. Every queue message must include the tenant_id. Every API response must include only the requesting tenant's data. The tenant_id is not a parameter — it is a security boundary enforced at the infrastructure layer (database, cache, queue) and validated at the application layer (middleware, service, repository).
performance_implications: Appending tenant_id to every query adds negligible overhead (the query planner handles the extra WHERE clause efficiently with a composite index on (tenant_id, ...)).

Dedicated connection pools per tenant isolate latency but multiply connection count by N tenants — for 100 tenants, 100 pools × 10 connections each = 1000 connections, each consuming ~10MB of PostgreSQL server memory.
Tenant-scoped caching (Redis keys prefixed with tenant_id) increases cache memory but prevents cross-tenant data leaks — each tenant's data is isolated in its own cache partition.
Tenant-aware connection pooling (routing queries by tenant_id to specific pool instances) adds ~5µs of routing overhead per query but prevents cross-tenant latency interference.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go developers building multi-tenant SaaS applications treat tenant_id as just another query parameter. They forget to add it to some queries, use the value from URL params instead of the authenticated session, and share connection pools across tenants. A single missing WHERE clause leaks every tenant's data to every other tenant — a P0 security incident. This lesson makes tenant isolation a cross-cutting concern enforced at every layer: middleware validates tenant_id, queries filter by tenant_id, caches include tenant_id in the key, and tests verify isolation with randomized data.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement a tenant isolation layer including: (1) a middleware that extracts tenant_id from a JWT and stores it in context, (2) a repository base type that automatically appends WHERE tenant_id = ? to every query, (3) a test that creates two tenants with overlapping data and proves that each tenant sees only their own data, and (4) a test that proves a user from tenant A cannot access tenant B's data even when guessing B's order IDs.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every SaaS platform implementing multi-tenancy depends on tenant isolation. GitHub uses organization-level isolation — every query includes the org ID. Slack uses workspace-level isolation. Opslane uses tenant-level isolation with a tenant_id in every table and every API response. A tenant leak at GitHub, Slack, or Opslane would be a P0 security incident with legal and regulatory consequences.
step_by_step_execution: User authenticates: receives JWT containing {user_id: 42, tenant_id: 'acme-corp'}
Request hits middleware: middleware decodes JWT, extracts tenant_id='acme-corp'
Middleware validates: checks that user_id 42 belongs to tenant 'acme-corp' (session integrity check)
Middleware stores: ctx = context.WithValue(ctx, tenantKey, 'acme-corp')
Handler receives context, passes to repository: repo.GetOrders(ctx, filters)
Repository extracts tenant from context: tenantID := ctx.Value(tenantKey).(string)
Repository scopes query: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE tenant_id = $1 AND ... — tenant_id is always the first filter
Service returns response: only orders belonging to 'acme-corp' are returned

Test: create tenant A with orders [1,2,3] and tenant B with orders [4,5,6], authenticate as tenant A, GET orders, verify only [1,2,3] are returned, verify GET /orders/4 returns 404 (not 200 with tenant B's order)
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Tenant isolation in Go is enforced at the repository layer using context-scoped values. The middleware stores the tenant ID in context using a typed key (type tenantKeyType struct{} to prevent key collisions). The repository base type (or a query builder wrapper) extracts the tenant ID and appends it to every SQL query's WHERE clause. For PostgreSQL, a composite index on (tenant_id, ...) ensures efficient filtering even with millions of rows per tenant. For Redis, cache keys include the tenant_id prefix (e.g., acme-corp:order:123). For message queues, the message header includes the tenant_id, and consumers filter by tenant_id before processing. Testing tenant isolation requires two tenants with identical data shapes and randomized data — the test asserts that queries scoped to tenant A return only A's data even when B's IDs are known.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Tenant isolation exists because multi-tenant SaaS platforms store data from multiple customers in the same database. Without explicit isolation at every data access layer, a single missing WHERE clause leaks all customers' data to all customers — a catastrophic security failure. Tenant isolation is not a feature — it is a security requirement enforced by architecture, code, and tests, not by hope.

items.title: Opslane order processing
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Opslane order processing in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Not using idempotency keys for payment processing — a network retry causes the payment gateway to charge the customer twice, and the duplicate charge is invisible until the customer reports it on their credit card statement.
Calling the payment gateway inside the HTTP handler without a timeout — a slow gateway blocks the handler for 60 seconds, exhausting the goroutine pool and causing all other requests to time out.
Acknowledging a queue message before processing completes — if the handler crashes after acknowledgement but before the order is persisted, the order is lost forever.

Assuming inventory reservation is synchronous — reserving inventory in a transaction that commits successfully does not guarantee the inventory is actually available if the reservation expires before fulfillment.
Logging credit card numbers or CVV in payment error logs — PCI DSS violation, immediate compliance failure, and the logs become a legal liability.
execution_timeline: Customer clicks 'Place Order' — frontend sends POST /orders with idempotency key 'ord-abc'
→ Server receives request: extracts idempotency key from header
→ Server checks idempotency store: key 'ord-abc' not found — first attempt
→ Server begins database transaction
→ Server validates inventory: all items in stock (SELECT with FOR UPDATE)
→ Server reserves inventory: UPDATE inventory SET quantity = quantity - 1 WHERE sku = 'XYZ' AND quantity > 0
→ Server calls payment gateway: POST /charge with idempotency key 'ch-pay-abc'
→ Payment gateway processes charge: holds $50 from customer's card
→ Payment gateway returns: {status: 'succeeded', charge_id: 'ch_789'}
→ Server records charge: INSERT INTO charges (order_id, charge_id, amount, status) VALUES (...) WITH charge-specific idempotency key
→ Server creates order: INSERT INTO orders (id, tenant_id, user_id, total, status) VALUES ('ord-abc', 'acme', 42, 50.00, 'confirmed')
→ Server commits transaction — all changes are durable
→ Server stores idempotency key: idempotency_keys('ord-abc') = 201 Created
→ Server returns 201 Created to frontend
failure_modes: Duplicate charge: the payment gateway charge succeeds but the response is lost — the client retries, the idempotency key is checked, but the first charge was never recorded in the local DB because the transaction rolled back after the charge — the second attempt also charges the card, double-charging the customer.

Inventory oversell: two concurrent orders both check inventory (both see 1 item in stock), both pass the check, both write to the order — the inventory goes to -1, and the second customer's order cannot be fulfilled even though the system accepted it.

Order lost after queue ack: a background worker dequeues an order message, writes to the database, acknowledges the message, and then crashes before confirming the write — the message is gone, the order is lost, and no alert fires.

Partial fulfillment: the payment succeeds but the order creation fails (DB constraint violation) — the customer is charged but has no order; the money is lost unless the system has a reconciliation job.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify the idempotency gap in a payment sequence diagram before seeing the corrected flow.
Learner must explain why a 200 OK with a stale order status is worse than a 500 error.
Learner must trace the order processing flow and identify which step is NOT covered by the idempotency key — revealing the payment-only-idempotency gap.
Learner must design the transaction boundary for an order that includes inventory reservation, payment, and order creation — choosing the correct rollback strategy for each failure mode.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Opslane's order processing follows the saga pattern: a sequence of local transactions with compensating actions. The order service manages the saga — it begins a local transaction, reserves inventory (local), calls payment (external with idempotency), creates the order (local), and commits. If payment fails, the inventory reservation is rolled back. If the order creation fails after payment, a compensating transaction refunds the payment. Each step has its own idempotency key, and the overall order has a top-level idempotency key for retry safety.
zero_magic.mental_model: Order processing is a saga — a sequence of steps where each step has a compensating action for rollback. Reserve inventory (compensate: release inventory), charge payment (compensate: refund), create order (compensate: cancel order). The saga must handle partial failures: if step 3 fails after step 2 succeeded, the saga runs the compensating action for step 2. The idempotency key at the top level ensures that retries do not create duplicate orders. The payment-specific idempotency key ensures that the payment gateway is not called twice for the same charge.
performance_implications: Each order processing step adds latency — a typical order with inventory check + payment + DB write takes 200-500ms. Use async processing for non-critical steps (email notification, analytics).
Payment gateway calls are the slowest step (100-500ms) — always call with a context deadline (10 seconds max) to avoid blocking the handler on a hung gateway.
Inventory reservation locks rows: SELECT FOR UPDATE on inventory rows blocks concurrent orders for the same SKU — keep the transaction short (under 1 second) to minimize contention on popular items.
Idempotency key storage in Redis adds ~1ms per lookup — negligible for order processing but critical for high-throughput payment events (webhooks).
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go developers building order processing systems treat the order as a single atomic operation. They do not consider partial failures (payment succeeds but order creation fails), duplicate charges (retry without idempotency), or inventory oversell (concurrent reservation without locking). This lesson covers the saga pattern for order processing: each step has a compensating action, the overall operation is idempotent, and the transaction boundary is carefully chosen to minimize the window for inconsistency.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement an order processing saga that: (1) reserves inventory with SELECT FOR UPDATE, (2) calls a simulated payment gateway with idempotency key, (3) creates the order in the database, (4) handles the case where payment succeeds but order creation fails (refunds the payment), (5) handles the case where the client retries with the same idempotency key (returns the existing order without re-charging). They must write a concurrent test proving that 10 simultaneous orders for the last item in stock result in exactly 1 success and 9 failures.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every e-commerce platform implements order processing as a saga. Shopify, Stripe, and Amazon all handle order processing with idempotency keys, inventory locking, and compensating transactions for payment refunds. Opslane's order processing must handle: (1) duplicate order creation (idempotency key), (2) payment failure after inventory reservation (release inventory), (3) inventory oversell under concurrent load (SELECT FOR UPDATE), and (4) payment gateway timeout (async reconciliation via webhook).
step_by_step_execution: Receive POST /orders with body {items: [{sku: 'XYZ', qty: 1}], idempotency_key: 'ord-abc'}
Check idempotency store for 'ord-abc' — not found, this is a new order
Begin database transaction (isolation: READ COMMITTED)
SELECT quantity FROM inventory WHERE sku = 'XYZ' FOR UPDATE — lock this row
Check quantity >= 1 — yes, proceed
UPDATE inventory SET quantity = quantity - 1 WHERE sku = 'XYZ'
Call payment gateway POST /charge with idempotency key 'pay-abc' — charge $50
Payment succeeds: store charge_id 'ch_789' in local state
INSERT INTO orders (id, tenant_id, user_id, total, status) VALUES ('ord-abc', 'acme', 42, 50.00, 'pending')
COMMIT transaction — all steps succeed atomically
Schedule async fulfillment: enqueue message 'fulfill order ord-abc'
If payment fails: ROLLBACK transaction — inventory reservation is released
If payment succeeds but order INSERT fails: ROLLBACK + call payment gateway refund with idempotency key 'refund-pay-abc'
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The saga pattern is implemented using local database transactions for local state and compensating API calls for external side effects. The local transaction handles inventory reservation and order creation atomically. The payment gateway call is outside the transaction (it cannot be inside a SQL transaction) — if it fails, the transaction is rolled back, releasing the inventory reservation. If the local transaction fails after payment succeeds, a refund is issued. The idempotency key is stored before the transaction begins (for the overall order) and before the payment call (for the payment). In Go, the saga is implemented as a state machine: OrderState transitions from Pending → PaymentPending → Confirmed → Fulfilled, with compensating transitions for each failure. The state machine is persisted in the database and retried by a background worker if the handler crashes mid-saga.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Order processing exists to coordinate multiple independent operations (inventory reservation, payment, order creation, fulfillment) into a reliable business transaction. Each operation has different failure semantics: inventory can be rolled back, payment can be refunded, and fulfillment can be retried. Without a saga pattern, a partial failure (payment succeeds but order fails) leaves the customer charged with no order — a data corruption that requires manual reconciliation. The saga pattern ensures that the system reaches a consistent state eventually, even under partial failures.

items.title: Opslane graceful shutdown
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Opslane graceful shutdown in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Closing the HTTP listener before draining active connections — in-flight requests are cut mid-execution, causing partial writes, corrupted responses, and client errors.

Using signal.Notify with an unbuffered channel — the signal is sent to the channel synchronously; if the channel is not ready to receive (main goroutine is busy), the signal is dropped and the process never shuts down.
Not setting a shutdown timeout — if a request hangs indefinitely, the server waits forever, and the rolling update never completes (minReadySeconds never satisfied).
Ignoring background goroutines in shutdown — the HTTP server shuts down gracefully, but background workers (log flushers, metric exporters, queue consumers) keep running or crash mid-operation.

Stopping the database connection pool before the HTTP server finishes draining — in-flight requests try to query the database, fail with 'pool closed', and produce 500 errors for the last few requests.
execution_timeline: SIGTERM received: orchestrator (Kubernetes, Nomad) sends SIGTERM to the Go process
→ signal.Notify channel receives the signal: main goroutine exits the select and calls server.Shutdown(ctx)
→ server.Shutdown closes the listener: no new connections accepted
→ server.Shutdown waits for active connections to finish: each in-flight request completes normally
→ server.Shutdown's context deadline (30 seconds) starts: active connections have 30 seconds to finish
→ Active connections complete: handler returns, response is fully written, connection is closed
→ server.Shutdown returns: all connections drained
→ Main goroutine closes database pool: db.Close() — waits for in-flight queries to complete, then closes all connections
→ Main goroutine signals worker goroutines: close(done) or cancel(ctx) — workers detect shutdown and exit
→ Main goroutine waits for worker completion: wg.Wait() (sync.WaitGroup) — all workers finished
→ Main goroutine exits: process terminates with exit code 0
failure_modes: Active request cut mid-write: server.Shutdown is called but the context deadline is too short (5 seconds) — a slow request (10 seconds) is terminated mid-response-write, and the client receives a truncated HTTP response body with no error status code.

Worker goroutine leak across deployments: a background worker does not listen for the shutdown signal — after 10 deployments, 10 leaked workers accumulate, each holding a database connection, exhausting the pool.

Shutdown deadlock: server.Shutdown waits for active requests, but those requests are blocked on a channel that is only drained by a background worker — the worker has already exited because it received the shutdown signal before the HTTP server finished draining. Circular wait → indefinite hang → deployment times out → process is SIGKILLed.

Signal channel overflow: two signals are sent in rapid succession (SIGHUP followed by SIGTERM) — the unbuffered channel can only hold one; the second signal is dropped, and the process does not shut down on SIGTERM.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must order the shutdown sequence for a multi-service process before seeing the working example.
Learner must explain why signal.Notify without a buffered channel can lose the shutdown signal.
Learner must identify the deadlock in a shutdown sequence where the HTTP server and a background worker depend on each other to complete.
Learner must design the WaitGroup usage for 3 background workers and the HTTP server — ensuring all goroutines complete before main exits.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's net/http provides http.Server.Shutdown(ctx) for graceful HTTP shutdown. It closes the listener, stops accepting new connections, and waits for active connections to complete (up to the context deadline). Go's os/signal package provides signal.Notify for catching OS signals. The idiomatic pattern is: sigCh := make(chan os.Signal, 1); signal.Notify(sigCh, syscall.SIGINT, syscall.SIGTERM); <-sigCh; cancel() (to propagate shutdown to all context-scoped goroutines); server.Shutdown(ctx); wg.Wait().
zero_magic.mental_model: Graceful shutdown is a coordinated sequence: stop accepting new work, wait for active work to complete, close shared resources, and exit. The order matters — you cannot close the database before the HTTP server finishes draining. Each goroutine must have a shutdown signal (context cancellation or channel close) and must complete within a deadline. The shutdown sequence is the inverse of the startup sequence: start workers last, stop them first.
performance_implications: Shutdown timeout should be based on the slowest acceptable request — if the slowest request is 10 seconds, set the Shutdown context to 15 seconds (10s + 5s margin).
WaitGroup overhead for tracking workers is negligible (~5ns per Add/Done call) — always use WaitGroup for tracking goroutine completion during shutdown.
signal.Notify with a buffered channel of size 1 is sufficient for signals — signals are idempotent (receiving SIGTERM twice is the same as once), and a buffer of 1 prevents losing the first signal.
Closing the database pool (db.Close()) waits for in-flight queries — set ConnMaxLifetime to ensure long queries are bounded; otherwise, db.Close() may wait indefinitely.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go developers deploy services without proper shutdown handling. When Kubernetes sends SIGTERM during a rolling update, the process exits immediately, cutting in-flight requests mid-execution and leaving partial state in the database. This lesson covers the full graceful shutdown sequence: signal handling, HTTP drain, worker coordination, and resource cleanup. The learner understands why a 30-second ShutdownTimeout exists and what happens during each phase of the shutdown sequence.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement a graceful shutdown sequence for a service that includes: (1) an HTTP server, (2) a background worker processing queue messages, (3) a database connection pool. The sequence must: listen for SIGINT/SIGTERM, call server.Shutdown with a 30-second timeout, signal workers via context cancellation, wait for workers to complete via WaitGroup, and close the database pool. They must write a test that sends SIGTERM during an in-flight request and proves the request completes successfully before the process exits.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every production Go service in Kubernetes depends on graceful shutdown. Kubernetes sends SIGTERM during rolling updates, scale-downs, and node drains. If the process does not handle SIGTERM, connections are cut, requests fail, and clients see 502 Bad Gateway from the load balancer. Opslane's graceful shutdown must handle: HTTP drain (30s timeout), background worker drain (queue messages are NACKed back to the queue if not processed), database connection close (pending queries complete or are canceled), and metrics flush (last batch of metrics is pushed before exit).
step_by_step_execution: Build: create a buffered signal channel: sigCh := make(chan os.Signal, 1)
Build: register signals: signal.Notify(sigCh, syscall.SIGINT, syscall.SIGTERM)
Build: create cancel context: ctx, cancel := signal.NotifyContext(context.Background(), syscall.SIGINT, syscall.SIGTERM) — this cancels ctx when a signal arrives (simpler than manual channel)
Build: start HTTP server in a goroutine: go func() { srv.ListenAndServe() }()
Build: start background workers with ctx: go worker(ctx, &wg)
Run: deploy to Kubernetes, wait for pod to be ready
Test: send a long-running request (10-second sleep) to the pod
Test: trigger a rolling update (kubectl rollout restart) — Kubernetes sends SIGTERM
Observe: server.Shutdown(ctx) waits for the 10-second request to complete
Observe: workers detect ctx.Done(), finish current task, mark WaitGroup as done
Observe: after all workers finish and server drains, db.Close() is called, process exits with 0
Verify: the long-running request completed successfully, no 502 from the load balancer
zero_magic.under_the_hood: signal.NotifyContext creates a context that is canceled when any of the specified signals arrive. Internally, it calls signal.Notify with a buffered channel and spawns a goroutine that listens on the channel and cancels the context. This is simpler than managing a raw signal channel manually. http.Server.Shutdown(ctx) closes the net.Listener, sets a flag on the server to reject new connections, and waits for the Server.activeConnections counter to reach 0. Active connections are tracked via a sync.WaitGroup — each connection's goroutine calls Add(1) on accept and Done() on close. When all connections complete (or the context deadline expires), Shutdown returns. If the deadline expires before all connections drain, Shutdown returns the context's error and the remaining connections are terminated. db.Close() closes the connection pool: it closes all idle connections immediately and waits for in-use connections to return to the pool before closing them (up to a timeout).
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Graceful shutdown exists because modern deployment systems (Kubernetes, Nomad, ECS) replace running processes frequently — rolling updates, scale-downs, and node drains happen multiple times per day. Without graceful shutdown, every replacement cuts active requests mid-execution, producing errors for real users. Graceful shutdown ensures zero-downtime deployments by coordinating the shutdown sequence: stop accepting new work, finish active work, close resources, and exit cleanly.
